reviews.summary:
This submission seeks to model the reactivation of brain activity during periods of quiescence using task-optimized network. The paper develops some mathematical formalisms to show that under certain assumptions, recurrent neural networks trained to perform a task develop denoising dynamics. 

While there are some interesting ideas presented in this submission, overall it feels that the results presented in the current version are preliminary and not surprising.
reviews.strengths:
— Understanding the neural mechanisms underlying reactions/replays is an important question.

-- The paper seeks to analyze RNNs trained to perform a class of tasks theoretically, which is a somewhat rare excise in this literature. 

— The mathematical analysis of the loss function used in the training and the connection to the Langevin dynamics, while under somewhat strong assumptions, remain interesting.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. A number of relevant studied were not cited and discussed. This include work using RNNs and attractor dynamics to model the replay and theta sequences in the hippocampus, e.g.,
Hopfield, John J. "Neurodynamics of mental exploration." Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 107.4 (2010): 1648-1653.
Kang, Louis, and Michael R. DeWeese. "Replay as wavefronts and theta sequences as bump oscillations in a grid cell attractor network." Elife 8 (2019): e46351.
Chu, Tianhao, et al. "Firing rate adaptation affords place cell theta sweeps, phase precession and procession." bioRxiv (2022): 2022-11.

The paper also misses several pieces work in training RNNs to study the grid cells systems and HD systems.
Cueva, C. J., & Wei, X. X. (2018). Emergence of grid-like representations by training recurrent neural networks to perform spatial localization. ICLR.
Uria, B., Ibarz, B., Banino, A., Zambaldi, V., Kumaran, D., Hassabis, D., Barry, C. and Blundell, C., (2020). The spatial memory pipeline: a model of egocentric to allocentric understanding in mammalian brains. BioRxiv, pp.2020-11.
Cueva, C.J., et al (2020). Emergence of functional and structural properties of the head direction system by optimization of recurrent neural networks.ICLR.

2. The main results are not surprising after considering what we know so far on this topic. Prior work has shown that training the RNNs to perform path integration or angular integration task leads to networks that exhibit attractors dynamics that is similar to continuous attractor models. Furthermore, it is well known that attractor dynamics can perform denoising. Thus it is not surprising that RNNs trained on these previous studied tasks can perform denoising. 

It should also be noted that prior work has further characterize the rate of diffusion along the low-d manifold, e.g., see Fig 6 of the following paper: 
Burak, Y. and Fiete, I.R., 2009. Accurate path integration in continuous attractor network models of grid cells. PLoS computational biology, 5(2), p.e1000291.


3. As described in the paper, the trained network follows diffusion dynamics when the stimulus turned off. This is naturally expected because the diffusion of network state on a low-d manifold. This would result in brownian-motion like trajectory. Furthemore, the replay trajectories observed in the hippocampus typically described by a systematic drift towards one direction (e.g., in linear or circular tracks), not diffusive dynamics. Can the authors be more explicit about the experimental data they were modeling?

4. One more point that confuses me—the authors stated “we found that even trajectories generated by networks that were not trained in the presence of noise, and also were not driven by noise in the quiescent phase, still generated quiescent activity distributions that corresponded well to the active phase distributions. ” This seems to argue against the denoising dynamics, and make the usefulness of the mathematical analysis questionable.

5. The mathematical analysis relied on a set of approximations and assumptions, which were not well justified.
reviews.questions:
Would it be possible to discuss more explicitly how their results are connected or supported by the empirical data? In particular, how is the “diffusive reactivation” related to neural data?

 The mathematical analysis relies on a number of assumptions, to the extent that it is difficult to judge whether the conclusion would actually be applicable to the numerical experiments. Can these assumptions be justified or better motivated (beyond the sake of mathematical convenience)?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This submission seeks to model the reactivation of brain activity during periods of quiescence using task-optimized network. The paper develops some mathematical formalisms to show that under certain assumptions, recurrent neural networks trained to perform a task develop denoising dynamics. 

While there are some interesting ideas presented in this submission, overall it feels that the results presented in the current version are preliminary and not surprising.

Strengths:
— Understanding the neural mechanisms underlying reactions/replays is an important question.

-- The paper seeks to analyze RNNs trained to perform a class of tasks theoretically, which is a somewhat rare excise in this literature. 

— The mathematical analysis of the loss function used in the training and the connection to the Langevin dynamics, while under somewhat strong assumptions, remain interesting.

Weaknesses:
1. A number of relevant studied were not cited and discussed. This include work using RNNs and attractor dynamics to model the replay and theta sequences in the hippocampus, e.g.,
Hopfield, John J. "Neurodynamics of mental exploration." Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 107.4 (2010): 1648-1653.
Kang, Louis, and Michael R. DeWeese. "Replay as wavefronts and theta sequences as bump oscillations in a grid cell attractor network." Elife 8 (2019): e46351.
Chu, Tianhao, et al. "Firing rate adaptation affords place cell theta sweeps, phase precession and procession." bioRxiv (2022): 2022-11.

The paper also misses several pieces work in training RNNs to study the grid cells systems and HD systems.
Cueva, C. J., & Wei, X. X. (2018). Emergence of grid-like representations by training recurrent neural networks to perform spatial localization. ICLR.
Uria, B., Ibarz, B., Banino, A., Zambaldi, V., Kumaran, D., Hassabis, D., Barry, C. and Blundell, C., (2020). The spatial memory pipeline: a model of egocentric to allocentric understanding in mammalian brains. BioRxiv, pp.2020-11.
Cueva, C.J., et al (2020). Emergence of functional and structural properties of the head direction system by optimization of recurrent neural networks.ICLR.

2. The main results are not surprising after considering what we know so far on this topic. Prior work has shown that training the RNNs to perform path integration or angular integration task leads to networks that exhibit attractors dynamics that is similar to continuous attractor models. Furthermore, it is well known that attractor dynamics can perform denoising. Thus it is not surprising that RNNs trained on these previous studied tasks can perform denoising. 

It should also be noted that prior work has further characterize the rate of diffusion along the low-d manifold, e.g., see Fig 6 of the following paper: 
Burak, Y. and Fiete, I.R., 2009. Accurate path integration in continuous attractor network models of grid cells. PLoS computational biology, 5(2), p.e1000291.


3. As described in the paper, the trained network follows diffusion dynamics when the stimulus turned off. This is naturally expected because the diffusion of network state on a low-d manifold. This would result in brownian-motion like trajectory. Furthemore, the replay trajectories observed in the hippocampus typically described by a systematic drift towards one direction (e.g., in linear or circular tracks), not diffusive dynamics. Can the authors be more explicit about the experimental data they were modeling?

4. One more point that confuses me—the authors stated “we found that even trajectories generated by networks that were not trained in the presence of noise, and also were not driven by noise in the quiescent phase, still generated quiescent activity distributions that corresponded well to the active phase distributions. ” This seems to argue against the denoising dynamics, and make the usefulness of the mathematical analysis questionable.

5. The mathematical analysis relied on a set of approximations and assumptions, which were not well justified.

Questions:
Would it be possible to discuss more explicitly how their results are connected or supported by the empirical data? In particular, how is the “diffusive reactivation” related to neural data?

 The mathematical analysis relies on a number of assumptions, to the extent that it is difficult to judge whether the conclusion would actually be applicable to the numerical experiments. Can these assumptions be justified or better motivated (beyond the sake of mathematical convenience)?

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

**Summary:**
This submission seeks to model the reactivation of brain activity during periods of quiescence using task-optimized network. The paper develops some mathematical formalisms to show that under certain assumptions, recurrent neural networks trained to perform a task develop denoising dynamics. Specifically, the authors propose that offline reactivation, characterized as "diffusive reactivation," can emerge as a consequence of optimal state variable estimation in noisy recurrent neural networks, particularly when integrating change-based sensory inputs. This framework is then numerically validated on spatial position and head direction estimation tasks.

While there are some interesting ideas presented in this submission, overall it feels that the results presented in the current version are preliminary and not surprising. The theoretical derivation, while intriguing, leads to outcomes that are largely anticipated given existing knowledge in the field, and its applicability to the full spectrum of observed biological reactivation phenomena remains limited.

**Strengths:**
— Understanding the neural mechanisms underlying reactions/replays is an important question. The paper tackles a significant problem in neuroscience, namely how brain circuits autonomously generate activity resembling waking states during quiescence, which is critical for functions like memory consolidation and learning.

-- The paper seeks to analyze RNNs trained to perform a class of tasks theoretically, which is a somewhat rare excise in this literature. Moving beyond purely empirical observations of emergent properties in RNNs, the work attempts to provide a mathematical foundation for how reactivation can arise from task optimization principles. This theoretical approach to understanding emergent neural phenomena is commendable.

— The mathematical analysis of the loss function used in the training and the connection to the Langevin dynamics, while under somewhat strong assumptions, remain interesting. The derivation linking the optimal denoising dynamics to Langevin sampling of the network's active state distribution (Eq. 19) offers a concise theoretical explanation for the observed similarity between active and quiescent state distributions (p(r) = ˜p(r)). This connection provides an elegant perspective on how intrinsic noise and task optimization can lead to a form of offline rehearsal.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  A number of relevant studied were not cited and discussed. This include work using RNNs and attractor dynamics to model the replay and theta sequences in the hippocampus, e.g.,
    Hopfield, John J. "Neurodynamics of mental exploration." Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 107.4 (2010): 1648-1653.
    Kang, Louis, and Michael R. DeWeese. "Replay as wavefronts and theta sequences as bump oscillations in a grid cell attractor network." Elife 8 (2019): e46351.
    Chu, Tianhao, et al. "Firing rate adaptation affords place cell theta sweeps, phase precession and procession." bioRxiv (2022): 2022-11.
    These papers, particularly Hopfield's work on neurodynamics of mental exploration and the studies by Kang & DeWeese and Chu et al. on theta sequences and wavefronts in grid cell networks, are highly pertinent as they discuss how specific forms of replay and sequential activity can emerge from network dynamics, including attractor mechanisms and firing rate adaptation. Given the manuscript's focus on emergent reactivation in RNNs, a discussion of these prior models would provide crucial context and allow for a clearer comparison of the proposed "diffusive reactivation" with more structured forms of replay observed in biological systems.

    The paper also misses several pieces work in training RNNs to study the grid cells systems and HD systems.
    Cueva, C. J., & Wei, X. X. (2018). Emergence of grid-like representations by training recurrent neural networks to perform spatial localization. ICLR.
    Uria, B., Ibarz, B., Banino, A., Zambaldi, V., Kumaran, D., Hassabis, D., Barry, C. and Blundell, C., (2020). The spatial memory pipeline: a model of egocentric to allocentric understanding in mammalian brains. BioRxiv, pp.2020-11.
    Cueva, C.J., et al (2020). Emergence of functional and structural properties of the head direction system by optimization of recurrent neural networks.ICLR.
    These studies are directly relevant to the numerical experiments presented in Section 3, as they demonstrate how grid-like and head direction cell representations, which form the basis of the tasks here, can emerge in RNNs through task optimization. Acknowledging and discussing these foundational works would strengthen the manuscript's literature review and contextualize its numerical findings within the established body of research on emergent representations in artificial neural networks. The current discussion in Section 5 only briefly touches upon prior work on grid cells and head direction systems in a general sense, without citing these specific and highly relevant RNN studies.

2.  The main results are not surprising after considering what we know so far on this topic. Prior work has shown that training the RNNs to perform path integration or angular integration task leads to networks that exhibit attractors dynamics that is similar to continuous attractor models. Furthermore, it is well known that attractor dynamics can perform denoising. Thus it is not surprising that RNNs trained on these previous studied tasks can perform denoising. The manuscript's claim that its work "provides theoretical support for modeling offline reactivation as an emergent consequence of task optimization in noisy neural circuits" (Abstract) and offers a "mathematical justification for the emergence of reactivation dynamics in terms of optimal task performance" (Discussion) is somewhat tempered by the existing understanding of continuous attractor networks. The numerical results, showing low-dimensional manifolds for spatial navigation (Fig. 1f-g) and a ring attractor for head direction (Fig. 3c-d), align well with the known properties of these types of networks. Given that attractor networks are inherently robust to noise and can perform denoising by moving states towards the attractor manifold, the emergence of denoising dynamics and subsequent diffusion along these manifolds during quiescence is an expected consequence rather than a novel, surprising finding.

    It should also be noted that prior work has further characterize the rate of diffusion along the low-d manifold, e.g., see Fig 6 of the following paper:
    Burak, Y. and Fiete, I.R., 2009. Accurate path integration in continuous attractor network models of grid cells. PLoS computational biology, 5(2), p.e1000291.
    The work by Burak & Fiete (2009) explicitly demonstrates and quantifies diffusion along attractor manifolds in grid cell models, including its dependence on noise levels. This suggests that the "diffusive reactivation" described here (Section 2.4, Eq. 19) is a known behavior of such systems under noise. While the specific derivation from an optimal loss function might be new, the phenomenon itself and some of its quantitative aspects have been explored in prior theoretical models, reducing the overall novelty of the emergent dynamics presented.

3.  As described in the paper, the trained network follows diffusion dynamics when the stimulus turned off. This is naturally expected because the diffusion of network state on a low-d manifold. This would result in brownian-motion like trajectory. Furthemore, the replay trajectories observed in the hippocampus typically described by a systematic drift towards one direction (e.g., in linear or circular tracks), not diffusive dynamics. Can the authors be more explicit about the experimental data they were modeling? The manuscript acknowledges this limitation in the Discussion, stating, "our theory does not yet explain how this phenomenon emerges" when referring to the moment-to-moment transition structure of waking networks. While Suppl. Fig. C.5 attempts to show some recapitulation of biased motion, the authors themselves note that "reversals in the trajectories still occurred," indicating that the systematic, temporally compressed or expanded, and often directional replay sequences observed in biological data (e.g., Nádasdy et al., 1999; Lee & Wilson, 2002; Foster, 2017) are not fully captured by the proposed diffusive dynamics. This discrepancy between the model's "diffusive reactivation" and the more structured, sequential "replay" observed in empirical neuroscience data represents a significant gap that needs to be more thoroughly addressed, either by explaining how diffusion could give rise to such structure or by clearly delineating the scope of reactivation phenomena the model aims to explain.

4.  One more point that confuses me—the authors stated “we found that even trajectories generated by networks that were not trained in the presence of noise, and also were not driven by noise in the quiescent phase, still generated quiescent activity distributions that corresponded well to the active phase distributions. ” This seems to argue against the denoising dynamics, and make the usefulness of the mathematical analysis questionable. This finding, presented in Section 4 and supported by Fig. 2e ("noiseless unbiased" and "noiseless biased" comparisons), directly challenges the necessity of the noise-driven denoising dynamics (Eq. 16, 19) as the *primary* mechanism for reactivation. If the correspondence between active and quiescent state distributions can emerge even in the complete absence of noise during both training and quiescence, it suggests that the learned attractor manifold itself, rather than the specific denoising computation, is sufficient to explain the distribution similarity. While the authors argue that noise enables "greater and continued exploration over long timescales" (Fig. 2f) and stabilizes activity (Suppl. Fig. C.4a-b), the fact that the core "reactivation phenomenon" (i.e., matching stationary distributions) persists without noise weakens the argument for the mathematical framework's unique explanatory power regarding the *emergence* of reactivation. This point needs clearer reconciliation with the theoretical claims.

5.  The mathematical analysis relied on a set of approximations and assumptions, which were not well justified. The derivation of the optimal dynamics (Section 2.3) and the emergent quiescent dynamics (Section 2.4) rests on several strong assumptions outlined in Section 2.1 and Supplementary Section B. For instance, the decomposition of `phi` into two functions (Eq. 4) is a significant structural assumption that may not hold for arbitrary RNN nonlinearities. The approximation `r(t) ≈ D†f(s(t))` used to simplify L2 (Eq. 10) assumes near-perfect reconstruction, which might not always be true, especially during initial training phases or in more complex tasks. Furthermore, the "greedy optimization" (Assumption B.4) is assumed to be a good approximation to globally optimal solutions, but its implications for network behavior in the presence of noise are not fully explored. Finally, the assumption of doubling noise variance during quiescence (Assumption B.8) to achieve exact Langevin sampling, while mathematically convenient, feels somewhat arbitrary from a biological perspective and is only partially mitigated by the claim that deviations are "not catastrophic." These assumptions, if not rigorously justified or shown to be generally applicable beyond mathematical convenience, limit the generality and biological relevance of the derived conditions for reactivation.

**Questions:**
Would it be possible to discuss more explicitly how their results are connected or supported by the empirical data? In particular, how is the “diffusive reactivation” related to neural data, especially given that biological replay often exhibits sequential structure, compression, and directional biases that differ from pure diffusion? Clarifying the specific aspects of empirical reactivation that the model aims to capture, and acknowledging the limitations regarding sequence replay, would strengthen the paper.

The mathematical analysis relies on a number of assumptions, to the extent that it is difficult to judge whether the conclusion would actually be applicable to the numerical experiments. Can these assumptions be justified or better motivated (beyond the sake of mathematical convenience)? For example, a more thorough discussion of the functional decomposition of network dynamics (Eq. 4) and the implications of the near-optimal state assumption (Eq. 10) would be beneficial.

**Rating:**
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This submission seeks to model the reactivation of brain activity during periods of quiescence using task-optimized network. The paper develops some mathematical formalisms to show that under certain assumptions, recurrent neural networks trained to perform a task develop denoising dynamics. 

While there are some interesting ideas presented in this submission, overall it feels that the results presented in the current version are preliminary and not surprising.

Strengths:
— Understanding the neural mechanisms underlying reactions/replays is an important question.

-- The paper seeks to analyze RNNs trained to perform a class of tasks theoretically, which is a somewhat rare excise in this literature. 

— The mathematical analysis of the loss function used in the training and the connection to the Langevin dynamics, while under somewhat strong assumptions, remain interesting.

Weaknesses:
1. A number of relevant studied were not cited and discussed. This include work using RNNs and attractor dynamics to model the replay and theta sequences in the hippocampus, e.g.,
Hopfield, John J. "Neurodynamics of mental exploration." Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 107.4 (2010): 1648-1653.
Kang, Louis, and Michael R. DeWeese. "Replay as wavefronts and theta sequences as bump oscillations in a grid cell attractor network." Elife 8 (2019): e46351.
Chu, Tianhao, et al. "Firing rate adaptation affords place cell theta sweeps, phase precession and procession." bioRxiv (2022): 2022-11.

The paper also misses several pieces work in training RNNs to study the grid cells systems and HD systems.
Cueva, C. J., & Wei, X. X. (2018). Emergence of grid-like representations by training recurrent neural networks to perform spatial localization. ICLR.
Uria, B., Ibarz, B., Banino, A., Zambaldi, V., Kumaran, D., Hassabis, D., Barry, C. and Blundell, C., (2020). The spatial memory pipeline: a model of egocentric to allocentric understanding in mammalian brains. BioRxiv, pp.2020-11.
Cueva, C.J., et al (2020). Emergence of functional and structural properties of the head direction system by optimization of recurrent neural networks.ICLR.

2. The main results are not surprising after considering what we know so far on this topic. Prior work has shown that training the RNNs to perform path integration or angular integration task leads to networks that exhibit attractors dynamics that is similar to continuous attractor models. Furthermore, it is well known that attractor dynamics can perform denoising. Thus it is not surprising that RNNs trained on these previous studied tasks can perform denoising. 

It should also be noted that prior work has further characterize the rate of diffusion along the low-d manifold, e.g., see Fig 6 of the following paper: 
Burak, Y. and Fiete, I.R., 2009. Accurate path integration in continuous attractor network models of grid cells. PLoS computational biology, 5(2), p.e1000291.


3. As described in the paper, the trained network follows diffusion dynamics when the stimulus turned off. This is naturally expected because the diffusion of network state on a low-d manifold. This would result in brownian-motion like trajectory. Furthemore, the replay trajectories observed in the hippocampus typically described by a systematic drift towards one direction (e.g., in linear or circular tracks), not diffusive dynamics. Can the authors be more explicit about the experimental data they were modeling?

4. One more point that confuses me—the authors stated “we found that even trajectories generated by networks that were not trained in the presence of noise, and also were not driven by noise in the quiescent phase, still generated quiescent activity distributions that corresponded well to the active phase distributions. ” This seems to argue against the denoising dynamics, and make the usefulness of the mathematical analysis questionable.

5. The mathematical analysis relied on a set of approximations and assumptions, which were not well justified.

Questions:
Would it be possible to discuss more explicitly how their results are connected or supported by the empirical data? In particular, how is the “diffusive reactivation” related to neural data?

 The mathematical analysis relies on a number of assumptions, to the extent that it is difficult to judge whether the conclusion would actually be applicable to the numerical experiments. Can these assumptions be justified or better motivated (beyond the sake of mathematical convenience)?

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Boosting Graph Anomaly Detection with Adaptive Message Passing

In particular, the model focuses on 2 types of anomalies: 1) contextual (a node having attributes that are very different from its neighbors') and 2) structural (dense subgraphs). The innovation is a two-stage model that first uses an MLP to find possible contextual anomalies and then uses a message-passing graph neural network with an attention mechanism to find dense subgraphs of anomalous nodes.

Summary:
The authors propose a model for unsupervised anomaly detection in graphs. In particular, the model focuses on 2 types of anomalies: 1) contextual (a node having attributes that are very different from its neighbors') and 2) structural (dense subgraphs). The innovation is a two-stage model that first uses an MLP to find possible contextual anomalies and then uses a message-passing graph neural network with an attention mechanism to find dense subgraphs of anomalous nodes.

Strengths:
- Takes inspiration of previous work on contrastive learning and recent work on attention for message passing 
- Competitive time complexity
- Experimental evaluation is fairly comprehensive and shows modest improvement over existing methods, of which there are plenty

Weaknesses:
- Performance reported for competing methods is lower than in the papers where these methods were proposed. It raises the question of whether the models are sufficiently tuned
- No comparison to methods in the vast literature outside deep learning / neural networks. See for example https://arxiv.org/abs/1404.4679

Questions:
- The AnomalyDAE paper reports an AUC of over 97 for the BlogCatalog dataset. Do you have an explanation for the discrepancy in your paper (AUC of 76.58)? Were the authors of that paper injecting anomalies in a totally different way?

- The AUC for Sub-CR, DOMINANT, and  COLA is higher in the Sub-CR paper. Do you think hyperparameters for the baseline models are sufficiently tuned?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**
The authors propose a model for unsupervised anomaly detection in graphs, named GADAM (Graph Anomaly Detection with Adaptive Message passing). The paper effectively identifies a crucial, often overlooked problem in existing methods: the inherent conflict between the smoothing effect of GNN message passing and the core intuition of local inconsistency mining (LIM) for anomaly detection. In particular, the model focuses on two main types of anomalies: 1) contextual anomalies, characterized by nodes having attributes that are very different from their neighbors', and 2) structural anomalies, which manifest as densely connected subgraphs in contrast to sparsely connected regular nodes (as illustrated in Figure 1). The innovation of GADAM lies in its clear and flexible two-stage framework that not only resolves this conflict but also leverages message passing to augment anomaly detection through a transformative approach to anomaly mining beyond LIM. The first stage employs an MLP-based approach to obtain local anomaly scores in a conflict-free manner, identifying possible contextual anomalies. The second stage then introduces a novel approach to capture anomaly signals from a global perspective, utilizing local anomaly scores as pseudo-labels to guide a message-passing graph neural network with an adaptive hybrid attention mechanism to discern global consistency and find dense subgraphs of anomalous nodes.

**Strengths:**
*   **Novel Approach to Conflict Resolution and Anomaly Mining:** The paper effectively addresses the "important but overlooked problem" of conflict between GNN message passing and the LIM intuition, which is a significant conceptual contribution. GADAM's two-stage design, which first disentangles message passing from LIM using an efficient MLP-based contrastive method (Section 3.1) and then integrates adaptive message passing in a global consistency discernment stage (Section 3.2), presents a principled solution. This design ensures conflict-free local anomaly score generation and then strategically uses message passing to enhance global anomaly signals, moving "beyond LIM" as stated in the abstract.
*   **Integration of Established Techniques:** The model takes inspiration from previous work on contrastive learning and recent work on attention for message passing. The first stage, for instance, leverages contrastive learning for LIM, building upon methods like CoLA (Section 2.2) but enhancing it with MLP as an encoder and sampling-free contrastive pair construction for improved efficiency and effectiveness. The second stage's adaptive message passing component incorporates a well-designed hybrid attention mechanism (Section 3.2.1) that considers both anomaly score differences and feature similarity, showing a thoughtful integration of attention-based approaches for fine-grained control of information flow. The global consistency discernment is also explicitly stated as being "Inspired by Chen et al. (2022)."
*   **Competitive Time Complexity and Scalability:** The proposed GADAM framework demonstrates competitive time complexity. The authors provide a detailed efficiency analysis in Section 3.3 and Appendix D, concluding that the overall time complexity is O((2f + f^2)N + 2E), which is linearly dependent on the number of nodes and edges in the graph. This is a significant practical advantage, especially for large-scale graphs. The experimental evaluation in Section 4.4 (Figure 3) visually confirms GADAM's superiority in both runtime and GPU overhead, excelling on large-scale OGB datasets like `ogbn-Arxiv` and `ogbn-Products` where many baselines encounter Out Of Memory (OOM) errors. This impressive scalability makes GADAM highly suitable for real-world applications with extensive graph data.
*   **Comprehensive Experimental Evaluation:** The experimental evaluation is fairly comprehensive and shows modest improvement over existing methods, of which there are plenty. The authors conduct extensive experiments on nine benchmark datasets (Section 4.1), including seven with injected synthetic anomalies and two with organic anomalies (Books and Reddit), covering a diverse range of graph characteristics and anomaly types. Performance is evaluated using standard metrics such as ROC-AUC, Average Precision, and Recall@k (Appendix B.5). The results in Table 1 demonstrate that GADAM achieves state-of-the-art performance on eight out of nine datasets and remains highly competitive on Pubmed, with reported ROC-AUC improvements ranging from 5.98% to 24.54% over baselines. The ablation studies (Section 4.5) further provide valuable insights into the contribution of the hybrid attention mechanism and the robustness of the pseudo-labeling strategy, showing consistent performance even with varying pseudo-label accuracy.

**Weaknesses:**
*   **Baseline Performance Discrepancies:** Performance reported for competing methods is consistently lower than in the papers where these methods were proposed. It raises the question of whether the models are sufficiently tuned. While the authors state they use the PyGod library or original source code (Section 4.1), the significant drops in performance for established baselines warrant further investigation or justification.
*   **Limited Scope of Baselines:** There is no comparison to methods in the vast literature outside deep learning / neural networks. The baselines primarily consist of autoencoder-based, contrastive learning-based, and GNN-specific methods (Section 4.1), all of which fall under the deep learning paradigm. See for example https://arxiv.org/abs/1404.4679 for examples of traditional graph anomaly detection techniques. Including a few representative non-neural network baselines would provide a more holistic view of GADAM's performance in the broader context of graph anomaly detection.

**Questions:**
*   The AnomalyDAE paper reports an AUC of over 97 for the BlogCatalog dataset. Do you have an explanation for the discrepancy in your paper (AUC of 76.58, as shown in Table 1)? Specifically, were the authors of that paper injecting anomalies in a totally different way? The manuscript states in Appendix A.1 and A.2 that for BlogCatalog, anomalies are "injected the same number of anomalies as the previous works," which suggests the injection method should be consistent.
*   Similarly, the AUC for Sub-CR, DOMINANT, and CoLA is higher in their respective original papers than what is reported in your Table 1 (e.g., Sub-CR on Cora is 0.9132, DOMINANT on Cora is 0.8493, and CoLA on Cora is 0.8801). Do you think hyperparameters for the baseline models are sufficiently tuned, or could there be other factors contributing to these consistent performance drops across multiple baselines?

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The authors investigate the two main paradigms, both used in many unsupervised graph anomaly detection models, message passings (MP) and local inconsistency mining (LIM). Traditionally, many graph anomaly detection models utilize the LIM for determining nodes that have inconsistency with their surroundings and mark them as anomalous. However, the use of MP with many GNN layers tends to smooth out everything, which decreases the contrastive difference between a node and its surroundings. The authors aim to address this conflict by proposing GADAM, a method that first performs LIM via a regular MLP network to get local anomaly scores without using message passing. It then combines these local scores with global scores that use an attention-based adaptive message passing that enables nodes to selectively absorb normal/abnormal signals from their surroundings. The authors then demonstrate the benefit of the models compared with baselines on several public datasets.
reviews.strengths:
Strengths:
- An interesting approach in unsupervised anomaly detection. The over-smoothing problem is a well-known problem in GNN, and it may impact the performance of GNN-based anomaly detection models. Most unsupervised graph anomaly detection models work by finding inconsistent nodes among the neighbors. The use of message passing in GNN may impact the finding as the over-smoothing problem may make the nodes less inconsistent.
I would argue that the conflict between LIM and overs-smoothing could be resolved within a message-passing framework by designing a better message-passing flow, imposing stricter bottlenecks in the encoder-decoder process, or other means. However, the paper proposed a different way outside the message-passing framework.

- The methods combine both local perspective and global perspective to create combined perspective anomaly scores.
- The authors demonstrate the benefit of the model against many GNN baselines
reviews.weaknesses:
I have a few concerns and questions regarding the paper:
1) In the contrastive pair construction (Section 3.1 item (2)), the authors mentioned that the method uses the complete adjacency subgraph. Does it mean that it includes all 1-hop neighbors? If that's the case, then the receptive field of the method is limited to just the immediate neighbors. This may limit the expressiveness of the model. There may be cases where determining normal/abnormal nodes requires more than just looking at the immediate neighbors. 
2) If in (1), it is not just 1-hop neighbors but all k-hop neighbors, then the size of the subgraphs can be potentially very large in densely connected graphs. How does the model address that?
3) In the shuffling process, there are some chances that neighboring nodes are accidentally picked as negative pairs. How does the model account for those cases?
4) The representations of a subgraph G in the local context are just an average of the representation of the node embeddings in G. Then, the anomaly scores for each node are measured by the difference between its node representation and the subgraph representation. It is possible that the node embedding of each neighbor is really different than the target node's embedding, but the average of the neighbor embeddings is similar to the target node. Doesn't it also introduce an over-smoothing problem? It could be worse than the standard message passing, where the aggregation is not just standard averaging but parameterized by a weight matrix. Here, the aggregation is just a plain average.
5) The use of attention mechanisms may be counter-intuitive in unsupervised anomaly detection that is based on finding abnormal signals from the surroundings. The ability of the attention mechanism to ignore certain signals may make the model ignore suspicious signals, which will result in an abnormal case tagged as a normal case. Has the author tried to replace the attention mechanism with another mechanism, like convolution, that forces the model to utilize all signals?
6) The experiment results of the baselines presented in the paper are rather different from the ones reported in the original paper for the same datasets. For example, in the original AnomalyDAE paper, the AUC are 97.81 (BlogCatalog), 90.05 (ACM); whereas in this paper, they are 76.58 (BlogCatalog), 75/16 (ACM). Could the authors explain more about it?
7) How to decide topk% included in the second stage. How does the selection of this percentage affect the results?
8) The paper is missing some unsupervised graph anomaly detection papers:
     - Ding, Kaize, Jundong Li, Nitin Agarwal, and Huan Liu. "Inductive anomaly detection on attributed networks." In Proceedings of the twenty-ninth international conference on international joint conferences on artificial intelligence, pp. 1288-1294. 2021.
     - Huang, Yihong, Liping Wang, Fan Zhang, and Xuemin Lin. "Are we really making much progress in unsupervised graph outlier detection? Revisiting the problem with new insight and superior method." arXiv preprint arXiv:2210.12941 (2022).
     - Yang, Shujie, Binchi Zhang, Shangbin Feng, Zhanxuan Tan, Qinghua Zheng, Jun Zhou, and Minnan Luo. "Ahead: A triple attention based heterogeneous graph anomaly detection approach." In Chinese Intelligent Automation Conference, pp. 542-552. Singapore: Springer Nature Singapore, 2023.
     - Fathony, Rizal, Jenn Ng, and Jia Chen. "Interaction-Focused Anomaly Detection on Bipartite Node-and-Edge-Attributed Graphs." In 2023 International Joint Conference on Neural Networks (IJCNN), pp. 1-10. IEEE, 2023.
     - Wang, Qizhou, Guansong Pang, Mahsa Salehi, Wray Buntine, and Christopher Leckie. "Cross-domain graph anomaly detection via anomaly-aware contrastive alignment." In Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, vol. 37, no. 4, pp. 4676-4684. 2023.
reviews.questions: Please answer my questions in the previous section.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

**Summary:**
The authors investigate the two main paradigms, both used in many unsupervised graph anomaly detection models, message passings (MP) and local inconsistency mining (LIM). Traditionally, many graph anomaly detection models utilize the LIM for determining nodes that have inconsistency with their surroundings and mark them as anomalous. However, the use of MP with many GNN layers tends to smooth out everything, which decreases the contrastive difference between a node and its surroundings. This is a crucial conflict identified in the paper, as GNNs typically aim to make connected nodes similar (Chai et al., 2022; Li et al., 2019a; Wu et al., 2019; Balcilar et al., 2021), directly clashing with the LIM intuition that anomalous nodes should be dissimilar to their neighbors. The authors aim to address this conflict by proposing GADAM, a novel framework that not only resolves this fundamental issue but also strategically leverages message passing to enhance anomaly detection. GADAM is a two-stage method that first performs LIM via a regular MLP network to get local anomaly scores without using message passing, thereby ensuring conflict-free local anomaly signal extraction. It then combines these local scores with global scores that use an attention-based adaptive message passing that enables nodes to selectively absorb normal/abnormal signals from their surroundings, transforming anomaly mining beyond simple local inconsistency. The authors then demonstrate the benefit of the models compared with baselines on several public datasets, including large-scale OGB graphs.

**Strengths:**
*   **An interesting approach in unsupervised anomaly detection.** The over-smoothing problem is a well-known problem in GNN, and it may impact the performance of GNN-based anomaly detection models. Most unsupervised graph anomaly detection models work by finding inconsistent nodes among the neighbors. The use of message passing in GNN may impact the finding as the over-smoothing problem may make the nodes less inconsistent. The paper effectively highlights this "important but overlooked problem" in Section 1, where GNNs' tendency to make connected nodes similar implicitly enhances consistency and makes anomalies indistinguishable. The proposed solution of employing an MLP-based approach for LIM in the first stage (Section 3.1) is a direct and efficient way to circumvent this conflict. I would argue that the conflict between LIM and over-smoothing could be resolved within a message-passing framework by designing a better message-passing flow, imposing stricter bottlenecks in the encoder-decoder process, or other means. However, the paper proposed a different way outside the message-passing framework, which is a notable departure from existing GNN-centric anomaly detection methods.
*   **The methods combine both local perspective and global perspective to create combined perspective anomaly scores.** GADAM’s two-stage framework is well-articulated, first obtaining `Slocal` through an MLP-based contrastive method for efficient and conflict-free local anomaly detection. Then, in the second stage, it converts the problem into a binary classification task using `Slocal` as pseudo-labels to guide the learning of global anomaly scores (`Sglobal`). This global perspective is achieved by discerning the alignment between nodes and a "global normal context" (Section 3.2.2) via an adaptive message passing scheme. The final anomaly score `S` is then a simple combination `(Slocal + Sglobal)/2`, offering a holistic measure that integrates both local inconsistencies and global deviations.
*   **The authors demonstrate the benefit of the model against many GNN baselines.** The experimental results presented in Section 4.2 and Table 1 show GADAM achieving significant improvements in ROC-AUC, ranging from 5.98% to 24.54% across nine benchmark datasets. Beyond effectiveness, the paper also highlights GADAM's impressive scalability and efficiency, excelling in both runtime and GPU memory consumption, particularly on large-scale OGB datasets where many baselines encounter Out-Of-Memory (OOM) errors (Figure 3, Table 1). This demonstrates its practical applicability for real-world large graphs.

**Weaknesses:**
I have a few concerns and questions regarding the paper:
1.  **In the contrastive pair construction (Section 3.1 item (2)), the authors mentioned that the method uses the complete adjacency subgraph.** Does it mean that it includes all 1-hop neighbors? If that's the case, then the receptive field of the method is limited to just the immediate neighbors. This may limit the expressiveness of the model. There may be cases where determining normal/abnormal nodes requires more than just looking at the immediate neighbors, especially for structural anomalies that might manifest as larger patterns or deviations in multi-hop neighborhoods, as illustrated conceptually in Figure 1. While the MLP-based approach avoids GNN smoothing, restricting the local context to only 1-hop neighbors might prevent the detection of more complex, higher-order structural anomalies.
2.  **If in (1), it is not just 1-hop neighbors but all k-hop neighbors, then the size of the subgraphs can be potentially very large in densely connected graphs.** How does the model address that? The paper states "adjacency subgraph of node vi" (Section 3.1), which typically implies 1-hop neighbors. If this interpretation is correct, then the issue of large k-hop subgraphs is avoided by design, but at the cost of the limited receptive field mentioned in the previous point. If it *were* to consider k-hop neighbors without sampling, the computational cost would indeed be substantial, especially for graphs with high average degrees like BlogCatalog or ogbn-Products (Table 4).
3.  **In the shuffling process, there are some chances that neighboring nodes are accidentally picked as negative pairs.** How does the model account for those cases? In Section 3.1, Eq. (4), the negative instance is formed by `⟨hlocal_i, Eshuf[i, :]⟩`, where `Eshuf` is obtained by shuffling `E` by row. `E` contains the embeddings of adjacency subgraphs. If, after shuffling, `Eshuf[i, :]` happens to be `E[j, :]` where node `j` is a neighbor of node `i`, then the model is effectively being asked to discriminate between node `i` and its *actual* neighbor's subgraph embedding as a negative pair. This could lead to noisy or incorrect learning signals for the contrastive loss, as a truly "negative" pair should ideally be from a non-neighboring, unrelated context.
4.  **The representations of a subgraph G in the local context are just an average of the representation of the node embeddings in G.** Then, the anomaly scores for each node are measured by the difference between its node representation and the subgraph representation. It is possible that the node embedding of each neighbor is really different than the target node's embedding, but the average of the neighbor embeddings is similar to the target node. Doesn't it also introduce an over-smoothing problem? It could be worse than the standard message passing, where the aggregation is not just standard averaging but parameterized by a weight matrix. Here, the aggregation is just a plain average (Section 3.1, "embedding of Gi^p is computed by averaging the node embeddings in Gi^p"). While this avoids the GNN-induced over-smoothing, the simplicity of averaging might itself dilute subtle local inconsistencies. If an anomalous node has only one highly distinct neighbor among many similar ones, a simple average might mask this individual inconsistency, making it harder to detect the anomaly. This could be viewed as a different form of information loss or smoothing at the local aggregation step.
5.  **The use of attention mechanisms may be counter-intuitive in unsupervised anomaly detection that is based on finding abnormal signals from the surroundings.** The ability of the attention mechanism to ignore certain signals may make the model ignore suspicious signals, which will result in an abnormal case tagged as a normal case. Has the author tried to replace the attention mechanism with another mechanism, like convolution, that forces the model to utilize all signals? The hybrid attention mechanism (Section 3.2.1) dynamically weights pre-attention (based on local anomaly score differences) and post-attention (based on feature similarity). While the paper argues this design enables "precise node-specific message passing," the concern remains that *selectively* emphasizing or de-emphasizing signals (as shown by attention coefficients, e.g., contextual anomalies having lower attention in Figure 6, Appendix E) might inadvertently filter out weak but meaningful anomaly cues. Even the "sharpening operation" for α < 0, while interesting, is still a form of selective processing rather than a comprehensive aggregation of all available signals, which a convolutional layer might offer.
6.  **The experiment results of the baselines presented in the paper are rather different from the ones reported in the original paper for the same datasets.** For example, in the original AnomalyDAE paper, the AUC are 97.81 (BlogCatalog), 90.05 (ACM); whereas in this paper, they are 76.58 (BlogCatalog), 75/16 (ACM). Could the authors explain more about it? This is a significant concern regarding the fairness and reproducibility of the comparisons. The substantial performance discrepancies, such as a drop of over 20 percentage points for AnomalyDAE on BlogCatalog, suggest that the experimental setup for baselines might differ considerably from their original implementations. While the paper states using PyGod or original source code (Section 4.1) and details anomaly injection (Appendix A.1), these differences could stem from various factors including: specific hyperparameter tuning for baselines, variations in data preprocessing, different anomaly injection parameters (`m`, `n` in Appendix A.1), or even subtle implementation details in the PyGod library compared to original codebases. Such large differences can make GADAM's improvements appear more pronounced than they might be under a truly consistent and optimized baseline comparison.
7.  **How to decide topk% included in the second stage. How does the selection of this percentage affect the results?** This question is well-addressed in the paper. Section 4.1 specifies the `kano` and `knor` percentages for establishing high-confidence normal and abnormal sets (e.g., `kano = 1, knor = 50` for Pubmed). Furthermore, Section 4.5.1 and Figure 4 provide an ablation study on the `kano` parameter. The findings indicate that while pseudo-label accuracy decreases with increasing `kano`, GADAM's performance remains robust, even when accuracy falls below 0.5. This suggests a degree of resilience to noisy pseudo-labels, which is a positive characteristic for an unsupervised method.
8.  **The paper is missing some unsupervised graph anomaly detection papers:**
    *   Ding, Kaize, Jundong Li, Nitin Agarwal, and Huan Liu. "Inductive anomaly detection on attributed networks." In Proceedings of the twenty-ninth international conference on international joint conferences on artificial intelligence, pp. 1288-1294. 2021. (AEGIS, briefly mentioned in Appendix B.4)
    *   Huang, Yihong, Liping Wang, Fan Zhang, and Xuemin Lin. "Are we really making much progress in unsupervised graph outlier detection? Revisiting the problem with new insight and superior method." arXiv preprint arXiv:2210.12941 (2022). (VGOD, briefly mentioned in Appendix B.4)
    *   Yang, Shujie, Binchi Zhang, Shangbin Feng, Zhanxuan Tan, Qinghua Zheng, Jun Zhou, and Minnan Luo. "Ahead: A triple attention based heterogeneous graph anomaly detection approach." In Chinese Intelligent Automation Conference, pp. 542-552. Singapore: Springer Nature Singapore, 2023. (AHEAD, briefly mentioned in Appendix B.4)
    *   Fathony, Rizal, Jenn Ng, and Jia Chen. "Interaction-Focused Anomaly Detection on Bipartite Node-and-Edge-Attributed Graphs." In 2023 International Joint Conference on Neural Networks (IJCNN), pp. 1-10. IEEE, 2023. (GraphBEAN, briefly mentioned in Appendix B.4)
    *   Wang, Qizhou, Guansong Pang, Mahsa Salehi, Wray Buntine, and Christopher Leckie. "Cross-domain graph anomaly detection via anomaly-aware contrastive alignment." In Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, vol. 37, no. 4, pp. 4676-4684. 2023. (ACT, briefly mentioned in Appendix B.4)
    While these relevant works are listed and briefly described in Appendix B.4, they are not included in the main experimental comparison (Tables 1, 6, 7). Given the rapid advancements in graph anomaly detection, omitting these recent and potentially strong baselines from the quantitative evaluation limits the comprehensiveness of the state-of-the-art comparison. For example, some of these, like AHEAD, are designed for specific graph types or settings that could be relevant here, or offer alternative perspectives on anomaly detection.

**Questions:**
Please answer my questions in the previous section.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Summary:
The authors investigate the two main paradigms, both used in many unsupervised graph anomaly detection models, message passings (MP) and local inconsistency mining (LIM). Traditionally, many graph anomaly detection models utilize the LIM for determining nodes that have inconsistency with their surroundings and mark them as anomalous. However, the use of MP with many GNN layers tends to smooth out everything, which decreases the contrastive difference between a node and its surroundings. The authors aim to address this conflict by proposing GADAM, a method that first performs LIM via a regular MLP network to get local anomaly scores without using message passing. It then combines these local scores with global scores that use an attention-based adaptive message passing that enables nodes to selectively absorb normal/abnormal signals from their surroundings. The authors then demonstrate the benefit of the models compared with baselines on several public datasets.

Strengths:
Strengths:
- An interesting approach in unsupervised anomaly detection. The over-smoothing problem is a well-known problem in GNN, and it may impact the performance of GNN-based anomaly detection models. Most unsupervised graph anomaly detection models work by finding inconsistent nodes among the neighbors. The use of message passing in GNN may impact the finding as the over-smoothing problem may make the nodes less inconsistent.
I would argue that the conflict between LIM and overs-smoothing could be resolved within a message-passing framework by designing a better message-passing flow, imposing stricter bottlenecks in the encoder-decoder process, or other means. However, the paper proposed a different way outside the message-passing framework.

- The methods combine both local perspective and global perspective to create combined perspective anomaly scores.
- The authors demonstrate the benefit of the model against many GNN baselines

Weaknesses:
I have a few concerns and questions regarding the paper:
1) In the contrastive pair construction (Section 3.1 item (2)), the authors mentioned that the method uses the complete adjacency subgraph. Does it mean that it includes all 1-hop neighbors? If that's the case, then the receptive field of the method is limited to just the immediate neighbors. This may limit the expressiveness of the model. There may be cases where determining normal/abnormal nodes requires more than just looking at the immediate neighbors. 
2) If in (1), it is not just 1-hop neighbors but all k-hop neighbors, then the size of the subgraphs can be potentially very large in densely connected graphs. How does the model address that?
3) In the shuffling process, there are some chances that neighboring nodes are accidentally picked as negative pairs. How does the model account for those cases?
4) The representations of a subgraph G in the local context are just an average of the representation of the node embeddings in G. Then, the anomaly scores for each node are measured by the difference between its node representation and the subgraph representation. It is possible that the node embedding of each neighbor is really different than the target node's embedding, but the average of the neighbor embeddings is similar to the target node. Doesn't it also introduce an over-smoothing problem? It could be worse than the standard message passing, where the aggregation is not just standard averaging but parameterized by a weight matrix. Here, the aggregation is just a plain average.
5) The use of attention mechanisms may be counter-intuitive in unsupervised anomaly detection that is based on finding abnormal signals from the surroundings. The ability of the attention mechanism to ignore certain signals may make the model ignore suspicious signals, which will result in an abnormal case tagged as a normal case. Has the author tried to replace the attention mechanism with another mechanism, like convolution, that forces the model to utilize all signals?
6) The experiment results of the baselines presented in the paper are rather different from the ones reported in the original paper for the same datasets. For example, in the original AnomalyDAE paper, the AUC are 97.81 (BlogCatalog), 90.05 (ACM); whereas in this paper, they are 76.58 (BlogCatalog), 75/16 (ACM). Could the authors explain more about it?
7) How to decide topk% included in the second stage. How does the selection of this percentage affect the results?
8) The paper is missing some unsupervised graph anomaly detection papers:
     - Ding, Kaize, Jundong Li, Nitin Agarwal, and Huan Liu. "Inductive anomaly detection on attributed networks." In Proceedings of the twenty-ninth international conference on international joint conferences on artificial intelligence, pp. 1288-1294. 2021.
     - Huang, Yihong, Liping Wang, Fan Zhang, and Xuemin Lin. "Are we really making much progress in unsupervised graph outlier detection? Revisiting the problem with new insight and superior method." arXiv preprint arXiv:2210.12941 (2022).
     - Yang, Shujie, Binchi Zhang, Shangbin Feng, Zhanxuan Tan, Qinghua Zheng, Jun Zhou, and Minnan Luo. "Ahead: A triple attention based heterogeneous graph anomaly detection approach." In Chinese Intelligent Automation Conference, pp. 542-552. Singapore: Springer Nature Singapore, 2023.
     - Fathony, Rizal, Jenn Ng, and Jia Chen. "Interaction-Focused Anomaly Detection on Bipartite Node-and-Edge-Attributed Graphs." In 2023 International Joint Conference on Neural Networks (IJCNN), pp. 1-10. IEEE, 2023.
     - Wang, Qizhou, Guansong Pang, Mahsa Salehi, Wray Buntine, and Christopher Leckie. "Cross-domain graph anomaly detection via anomaly-aware contrastive alignment." In Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, vol. 37, no. 4, pp. 4676-4684. 2023.

Existing methods often conflict in their focus between local inconsistency mining (LIM) and message passing. LIM emphasizes identifying high similarities between abnormal nodes and their neighbors, while message passing, commonly employed by graph neural networks (GNNs), tends to make connected nodes similar, leading to local anomaly signal loss. To reconcile this conflict, the authors propose GADAM, a novel framework that not only resolves the tension between LIM and message passing but also utilizes message passing to enhance anomaly detection through a unique approach to anomaly mining beyond LIM. The effectiveness and efficiency of GADAM are extensively evaluated on nine benchmark datasets, including two large-scale OGB datasets. The results demonstrate that GADAM outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, showcasing superior effectiveness and efficiency in unsupervised graph anomaly detection.
reviews.strengths:
- The proposed method is technically sound, i.e., to overcome the issues of LIM in previous anomaly detection methods.

The influence of different types of anomalies on the proposed method is not discussed.

2. If there is no prior information about the ratio of anomalous nodes in a given graph, how should one select k_{ano}? If the selected k leads to more anomalies than actual ones, will the proposed method be impacted negatively?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper, the authors address the challenge of unsupervised graph anomaly detection, a crucial task in various real-world applications. Existing methods often conflict in their focus between local inconsistency mining (LIM) and message passing. LIM emphasizes identifying high similarities between abnormal nodes and their neighbors, while message passing, commonly employed by graph neural networks (GNNs), tends to make connected nodes similar, leading to local anomaly signal loss. To reconcile this conflict, the authors propose GADAM, a novel framework that not only resolves the tension between LIM and message passing but also utilizes message passing to enhance anomaly detection through a unique approach to anomaly mining beyond LIM. The effectiveness and efficiency of GADAM are extensively evaluated on nine benchmark datasets, including two large-scale OGB datasets. The results demonstrate that GADAM outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, showcasing superior effectiveness and efficiency in unsupervised graph anomaly detection.

Strengths:
- The proposed method is technically sound, i.e., to overcome the issues of LIM in previous anomaly detection methods.
- The proposed method has been tested on both synthetic and real-world anomalies and the experimental results show the effectiveness of the proposed method.
- The conducted experiments are comprehensive including performance comparison, efficiency comparison, and ablation studies.

Weaknesses:
- Although the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed method have been empirically studied from different aspects, this is no theoretical guarantee of the proposed method.
- The first step of MLP-based LIM aims to identify local anomalies only. However, if the given data contains more contextual anomalies, will the proposed method fail to capture them?
The influence of different types of anomalies on the proposed method is not discussed.

Questions:
1. How to calculate H_{global} in details?
2. If there is no prior information about the ratio of anomalous nodes in a given graph, how should one select k_{ano}? If the selected k leads to more anomalies than actual ones, will the proposed method be impacted negatively?
3. What are the detailed steps in injecting anomalies? What the performance will change if different numbers of contextual or structural anomalies are injected (especially the proposed method first detects local structural anomalies)?

--------------------------

After rebuttal, my concerns have been addressed.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**
In this paper, the authors address the critical challenge of unsupervised graph anomaly detection (UGAD), a task with significant implications across various real-world applications such as financial fraud detection and social network analysis. They keenly observe that existing methods often grapple with a fundamental conflict between local inconsistency mining (LIM) and the inherent smoothing effect of message passing in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). While LIM operates on the intuition that anomalous nodes are difficult to align with their neighbors due to their distinct characteristics, GNNs' message passing mechanisms inherently tend to make connected nodes more similar, thereby risking the loss of crucial local anomaly signals. To effectively reconcile this conflict, the authors propose GADAM (Graph Anomaly Detection with Adaptive Message passing), a novel and flexible two-stage framework. GADAM not only systematically resolves the tension between LIM and message passing but also strategically leverages message passing to augment anomaly detection through a transformative approach to anomaly mining that extends beyond traditional LIM. Specifically, the first stage introduces an efficient MLP-based LIM approach, designed to obtain local anomaly scores in a conflict-free manner. The second stage then employs a hybrid attention-based adaptive message passing mechanism, enabling nodes to selectively absorb signals from their surroundings, complemented by a global consistency discernment module that uses pseudo-labels derived from the first stage. Extensive experiments conducted on nine benchmark datasets, including seven with injected synthetic anomalies and two large-scale OGB datasets (ogbn-arxiv and ogbn-products) with organic anomalies, demonstrate that GADAM consistently surpasses existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of both effectiveness and efficiency in unsupervised graph anomaly detection.

**Strengths:**
*   The proposed method is technically sound, offering a well-reasoned approach to overcome the persistent issues of local inconsistency mining (LIM) in previous anomaly detection methods. By explicitly decoupling message passing from the initial LIM stage using an MLP encoder (Section 3.1), GADAM effectively avoids the "smoothing effect" problem identified in prior GNN-based approaches. Furthermore, the subsequent integration of adaptive message passing (Section 3.2.1) and global consistency discernment (Section 3.2.2) provides a sophisticated mechanism to then leverage graph structure for enhanced anomaly detection, demonstrating a clear and flexible framework.
*   The proposed method has been thoroughly tested on both synthetic and real-world anomalies, and the experimental results consistently demonstrate the effectiveness of GADAM. The paper utilizes a comprehensive set of nine benchmark datasets, detailed in Section 4.1 and Appendix A, which include widely-used citation networks (Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed, ACM), social networks (BlogCatalog), and large-scale OGB datasets (ogbn-arxiv, ogbn-products), as well as datasets with organic anomalies (Books, Reddit). Notably, Table 1 highlights GADAM's remarkable ROC-AUC improvements, ranging from 5.98% to 24.54% across these diverse datasets, showcasing its robust performance across various anomaly types and graph characteristics.
*   The conducted experiments are comprehensive, covering performance comparison, efficiency comparison, and detailed ablation studies. The performance evaluation (Section 4.2, Table 1, 6, 7) utilizes standard metrics like ROC-AUC, Average Precision, and Recall@k, providing a holistic view of the model's capabilities. The efficiency comparison (Section 4.4, Figure 3) rigorously analyzes both runtime and GPU memory overhead, demonstrating GADAM's superior scalability, particularly on large OGB datasets where many baselines encounter Out-Of-Memory (OOM) errors. Furthermore, the ablation studies (Section 4.5) provide valuable insights into the contribution of key components, such as the size and quality of the high-confidence anomaly set (Figure 4) and the hybrid attention mechanism (Table 3), confirming their impact on the model's overall efficacy. The analysis of embedding dimension (Figure 5) also helps justify hyperparameter choices.

**Weaknesses:**
*   Although the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed method have been empirically studied from different aspects, there is no theoretical guarantee of the proposed method initially presented in the main paper. While the empirical results are strong, a formal theoretical analysis, especially for the global consistency discernment stage that leverages pseudo-labels and adaptive message passing, would further strengthen the paper's contribution and provide deeper insights into GADAM's robustness and convergence properties.
*   The first step of MLP-based LIM aims to identify local anomalies only. However, if the given data contains more contextual anomalies, will the proposed method fail to capture them effectively in this initial stage? The `s_local` score (Equation 6) is derived from the feature similarity between a node and its adjacent subgraph. If contextual anomalies primarily manifest through feature differences not easily captured by this local similarity (e.g., subtle feature shifts over a broader neighborhood, or if their immediate neighbors also share some anomalous features), the MLP-based LIM might struggle to accurately assign high initial anomaly scores, potentially impacting the quality of pseudo-labels for the second stage.
*   The influence of different types of anomalies (contextual vs. structural, as defined in Figure 1 and Section 1) on the proposed method is not discussed in detail within the main experimental results. While the overall performance on datasets with injected anomalies is provided, a breakdown of how GADAM performs specifically on contextual versus structural anomalies would offer a more nuanced understanding of its strengths and limitations for each anomaly type. This is particularly relevant given the paper's initial premise of addressing the conflict for both types.

**Questions:**
1.  How is `H_global` calculated in detail? Section 3.2, "Hybrid Attention based Adaptive Message Passing," states, "We first employ another MLP and follow Eq. (3) for all nodes to get Hglobal in the second stage." While this suggests a similar MLP structure to the first stage, further clarification on its specific initialization, whether it's trained from scratch, or if it leverages weights from the first stage's MLP, would be beneficial for reproducibility and understanding its role.
2.  If there is no prior information about the ratio of anomalous nodes in a given graph, how should one select `k_ano`? The paper mentions setting `kano = 1` or `5` for specific datasets (Section 4.1, "Implementation details"), but this assumes some prior knowledge or a tuning process. If the selected `k_ano` leads to more anomalies being labeled as pseudo-anomalies than actual ones (i.e., a high false positive rate in the pseudo-labeling stage), will the proposed method be impacted negatively, especially since the global consistency discernment relies on these pseudo-labels? Although Section 4.5.1 and Figure 4 demonstrate robustness to `k_ano` variations and even low pseudo-label accuracy, the initial heuristic for choosing `k_ano` in a completely unsupervised setting without any prior knowledge remains a practical concern.
3.  What are the detailed steps in injecting anomalies? What the performance will change if different numbers of contextual or structural anomalies are injected (especially considering the proposed method first detects local structural anomalies)? Appendix A.1 provides a good overview of the contextual and structural anomaly injection methods. However, given the paper's emphasis on distinguishing between these two types in the introduction (Figure 1), a detailed analysis of GADAM's performance under varying proportions or dominant types of injected anomalies would be valuable. For instance, how does the model perform if 80% of anomalies are contextual and 20% are structural, versus the inverse? This would provide deeper insights into the effectiveness of the two-stage approach for each anomaly category.

**After rebuttal:**
The authors have provided a theoretical guarantee for the second stage of GADAM, which is included in Appendix F. Furthermore, Appendix G offers a detailed analysis of GADAM's detection performance on structural, contextual, and combined anomalies, directly addressing the concerns raised regarding the influence of different anomaly types. These additions significantly strengthen the paper.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper addresses the problem of unsupervised node-level anomaly detection in a graph through an adaptive message passing framework. The paper rightly motivates the fact that message passing in GNNs results in local anomaly signal loss. The authors have proposed an approach to obtain local and global anomaly scores through MLP and hybrid attention based adaptive message passing. The paper has conducted experiments on anomaly detection on real world datasets with both injected and ground truth anomalies, and used a good number of baseline approaches from the literature.
reviews.strengths:
1. The idea of using local anomaly score to compute the global anomaly score is interesting.
2. The paper uses a good number of baseline algorithms from the literature.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The problem formulation seems to be problematic. Authors have mentioned "Structural anomalies are densely connected nodes in contrast to sparsely connected regular nodes". What about the networks that are dense by nature? Moreover, if density is the only criteria for being a structural anomaly, why can't we use degree of a node as the metric to find the structural anomalies? Are we over-complicating the solution of a relatively simple problem?

2. I am not convinced with the proposed solution. s^local (in Eq. 6) can only capture contextual anomalies, but not the structural anomalies because of the L2 normalization in Eq. 3. Where exactly are we capturing the structural anomalies in this framework?

3. What is the difference between H_local and H_global? They seem to be computed in the same way through Eq. 3. Why are we calling the second one as "global"?

4. Some of the claims in the paper should have been explained more. Can you please elaborate the reason of "as the training advances, the influence of pre-attention gradually diminishes, while the opposite for post-attention".

5. In Table 1, overall anomaly detection performance is reported. I am curious to see the performance on contextual and structural anomalies separately.

6. As mentioned before, degree centrality seems to be a good metric to capture the structural anomalies. Can you please include this heuristic as a baseline to compare with?

7. How do you capture the nodes which are both contextual and structural anomalies? Also, will your approach be able to capture "Combined Outliers" (or combined anomalies) as introduced in "Outlier Aware Network Embedding for Attributed Networks" (AAAI-2019)?
reviews.questions:
Please see the questions above.

***********************************

After Rebuttal: The authors have tried to address all the concerns I had. I am changing my score from 3 to 5. I believe the paper needs a major revision to incorporate the recommended changes and the new experiments. I don't mind if this paper gets accepted in ICLR 2024 with those changes.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
