reviews.summary:
This purely theoretical paper is devoted to formal explainability of
sequential ML models, e.g. RNNs and Transformers. Namely, the paper
lifts the apparatus of formal XAI (FXAI) to sequential models and
studies the problem of checking whether there exists a single formal
abductive explanation or a single formal contrastive explanation for
such a model's prediction and offers a few theoretical results
demonstrating the complexity of this problem. Then the authors discuss
the complexity of the same problem for various types of automata
showing some negative (intractability) and some positive (polytime)
results in this domain.
reviews.strengths:
- This paper discusses the complexity of testing whether there exists
  a formal explanation for a sequential ML model. Although this is not
  the first work on the subject (I've already reviewed a few this
  year), but the theoretical contributions seem clearly novel to me.

- The paper looks rigorous in terms of the problems studied and
  propositions claimed. I haven't checked the proofs but the results
  make sense to me.
reviews.weaknesses:
- This is a purely theoretical paper with a list of propositions and
  proof sketches but with absolutely no practical side of things. No
  experimental evidence is provided either. As a result, whether or
  not FXAI is usable in the context of such models is left unclear.
  (Recall that AXp's and CXp's are known to be in general intractable
  to compute for non-sequential models; so no big deal.) This makes me
  wonder why this line of work can be deemed relevant for a conference
  like ICLR. (KR would be a nice fit but ICLR is hard to justify.)

- On a similar note: overall presentation could and should be
  significantly improved. Definitions of abductive and contrastive
  explanations in the context of sequential models are given in a
  somewhat lazy way. (The models are defined similarly.) Also, the
  paper gives no examples whatsoever that would demonstrate how these
  explanations look like for sequential models. As this paper claims
  to be the first of its kind, such examples should be provided.

- No algorithms to be used for computing such explanations are
  proposed (even in theory). This exacerbates the issue of the venue
  choice above.

- Minor: on p9 there is a sentence: "This type of explainability falls
  within the category of post-hoc local model-agnostic explanations".
  No, it does not. Both abductive and contrastive explanations are
  global. None of them are model-agnostic as you have to reason about
  the model's behavior formally and hence require access to its
  original representation.
reviews.questions:
- Can you show a clear example of an RNN model, some of the instances
  it takes as input and also exemplify abductive and contrastive
  explanations for some of the model's decisions?

- Are the algorithms originally proposed for AXp and CXp computation
  are directly usable in this setting? If not, how should one compute
  them?

- Can you summarize the differences between the complexity of these
  problems for non-sequential and sequential models? This way, can you
  contrast your results with what is already known to hold in general?

- Why do you need 1 ** k in the informal definition of a CXp? It
  doesn't seem to be used anywhere, does it?

Summary:
This purely theoretical paper is devoted to formal explainability of
sequential ML models, e.g. RNNs and Transformers. Namely, the paper
lifts the apparatus of formal XAI (FXAI) to sequential models and
studies the problem of checking whether there exists a single formal
abductive explanation or a single formal contrastive explanation for
such a model's prediction and offers a few theoretical results
demonstrating the complexity of this problem. Then the authors discuss
the complexity of the same problem for various types of automata
showing some negative (intractability) and some positive (polytime)
results in this domain.

Strengths:
- This paper discusses the complexity of testing whether there exists
  a formal explanation for a sequential ML model. Although this is not
  the first work on the subject (I've already reviewed a few this
  year), but the theoretical contributions seem clearly novel to me.

- The paper looks rigorous in terms of the problems studied and
  propositions claimed. I haven't checked the proofs but the results
  make sense to me.

Weaknesses:
- This is a purely theoretical paper with a list of propositions and
  proof sketches but with absolutely no practical side of things. No
  experimental evidence is provided either. As a result, whether or
  not FXAI is usable in the context of such models is left unclear.
  (Recall that AXp's and CXp's are known to be in general intractable
  to compute for non-sequential models; so no big deal.) This makes me
  wonder why this line of work can be deemed relevant for a conference
  like ICLR. (KR would be a nice fit but ICLR is hard to justify.)

- On a similar note: overall presentation could and should be
  significantly improved. Definitions of abductive and contrastive
  explanations in the context of sequential models are given in a
  somewhat lazy way. (The models are defined similarly.) Also, the
  paper gives no examples whatsoever that would demonstrate how these
  explanations look like for sequential models. As this paper claims
  to be the first of its kind, such examples should be provided.

- No algorithms to be used for computing such explanations are
  proposed (even in theory). This exacerbates the issue of the venue
  choice above.

- Minor: on p9 there is a sentence: "This type of explainability falls
  within the category of post-hoc local model-agnostic explanations".
  No, it does not. Both abductive and contrastive explanations are
  global. None of them are model-agnostic as you have to reason about
  the model's behavior formally and hence require access to its
  original representation.

Questions:
- Can you show a clear example of an RNN model, some of the instances
  it takes as input and also exemplify abductive and contrastive
  explanations for some of the model's decisions?

- Are the algorithms originally proposed for AXp and CXp computation
  are directly usable in this setting? If not, how should one compute
  them?

- Can you summarize the differences between the complexity of these
  problems for non-sequential and sequential models? This way, can you
  contrast your results with what is already known to hold in general?

- Why do you need 1 ** k in the informal definition of a CXp? It
  doesn't seem to be used anywhere, does it?

reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

**Summary:**
This purely theoretical paper is devoted to formal explainability of sequential ML models, e.g., Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Transformers, as well as various automata models. Namely, the paper lifts the apparatus of formal Explainability in AI (FXAI) to sequential models, which traditionally focused on non-sequential models like MLPs and decision trees. It specifically studies the computational complexity of checking whether there exists a single formal abductive explanation (also known as minimum sufficient reasons) or a single formal counterfactual explanation (also known as contrastive explanations or minimum change required) for such a model's prediction. The paper offers a few theoretical results demonstrating the complexity of this problem across different model classes. For popular neural sequential models like RNNs and Transformers, the authors observe that both abductive and counterfactual explanations are computationally intractable, often NP-hard or even undecidable. Then, the discussion shifts to models from formal language theory, where the paper explores the complexity for various types of automata. Here, the authors present some negative results, such as PSPACE-completeness for abductive explanations for Deterministic Finite Automata (DFA) with regular language features, but also provide positive results, demonstrating polynomial-time solvability for counterfactual explanations for DFA, deterministic interval automata, and deterministic k-register automata (for fixed k).

**Strengths:**
*   This paper discusses the complexity of testing whether there exists a formal explanation for a sequential ML model, a domain where FXAI has previously been largely unexplored, as highlighted in the introduction (Page 1: "Hitherto, the primary focus of FXAI has been on non-sequential models"). Although this is not the first work on the broader subject of FXAI, the theoretical contributions are clearly novel in extending this framework to the critical area of sequential data and models. The identification of complexity bounds for various sequential architectures, from advanced neural networks to more constrained automata, represents a significant step forward in understanding the fundamental limits of explainability in this context.
*   The paper looks rigorous in terms of the problems studied and propositions claimed. The formal definitions of sequential models, explanation types, and distance measures (Hamming, Edit, DTW) are clearly laid out in Section 2 and 3. The complexity results, spanning from NP-hard and undecidable for complex models like RNNs and Transformers (Proposition 1) to PSPACE-hard for DFA abductive explanations (Proposition 2) and PTIME for automata-based counterfactuals (Theorem 1, 2), appear well-supported by the provided proof sketches. The detailed discussion of complexity classes and measures further underscores this rigor. I haven't checked the proofs in their entirety but the results, particularly the intractability for powerful neural models and the more nuanced picture for automata, make strong theoretical sense to me.

**Weaknesses:**
*   This is a purely theoretical paper with a list of propositions and proof sketches but with absolutely no practical side of things. No experimental evidence is provided either. As a result, whether or not FXAI is usable in the context of such models is left unclear, particularly given the strong negative results for RNNs and Transformers (Proposition 1). While the paper identifies polynomial-time algorithms for counterfactuals in certain automata models (Theorem 1 and 2), these are presented at a high theoretical level (e.g., reduction to shortest-path problems on weighted graphs) without any discussion of their practical implementation, scalability, or empirical performance. This raises significant questions about the tangible impact for a conference like ICLR, which typically emphasizes practical advancements and empirical validation in machine learning. (Recall that abductive explanations (AXp's) and counterfactual explanations (CXp's) are known to be in general intractable to compute for non-sequential models; so no big deal that they are for sequential models as well.) This makes me wonder why this line of work can be deemed relevant for a conference like ICLR, as its primary appeal seems to be to the theoretical computer science or knowledge representation (KR) communities. KR would be a nice fit but ICLR is hard to justify without a clearer path to practical application or empirical demonstration.
*   On a similar note: overall presentation could and should be significantly improved. Definitions of abductive and contrastive explanations in the context of sequential models, while formally stated, are given in a somewhat lazy way. For instance, the definition of an abductive explanation on page 4 is dense and could benefit from a more intuitive, step-by-step breakdown for a broader ML audience. Similarly, the models are defined concisely but without much pedagogical scaffolding. Although the paper does provide some examples (e.g., Example 1 for abductive explanations in document classification, and Examples 2 and 3 for counterfactuals in stock trading with interval and register automata), these are either abstract or tied to specific automata models. The paper gives no sufficiently clear or demonstrative examples that would truly illustrate how these explanations look like for the more complex and widely used neural sequential models like RNNs and Transformers, for which the main negative results are derived. As this paper claims to be the first of its kind to systematically study FXAI for sequential models, such concrete, illustrative examples for the most challenging cases should be provided to ground the theoretical discussion.
*   No algorithms to be used for computing such explanations are proposed for the computationally intractable cases, which include RNNs, Transformers, and even abductive explanations for DFA (Proposition 1 and 2). For the tractable cases (counterfactuals for DFA, interval automata, k-DRA), while theoretical polynomial-time algorithms are outlined (e.g., reduction to shortest path problems in weighted graphs in the proofs of Theorem 1 and 2), these are high-level descriptions rather than concrete, implementable algorithms. There is a notable absence of pseudo-code, complexity analysis beyond the theoretical "polynomial-time," or discussion of practical considerations (e.g., memory requirements for graph construction for large inputs). This exacerbates the issue of the venue choice mentioned above, as ICLR readers would expect at least a conceptual framework for practical computation, even if it's for the "interpretable" subset of models.
*   Minor: on p9 there is a sentence: "This type of explainability falls within the category of post-hoc local model-agnostic explanations". No, it does not. Both abductive and contrastive explanations, as defined and studied in this paper and the broader FXAI literature, are inherently model-specific. The methodology relies on reasoning formally about the model's internal structure and behavior (e.g., transitions in an automaton, or weights in an MLP as referenced in related work), which directly contradicts the definition of model-agnosticism. While they are indeed local (for a specific input 'v') and post-hoc (after the model has been trained), their generation requires deep access to the model's original representation, making them distinctly model-specific. This miscategorization should be corrected for clarity and accuracy within the XAI landscape.

**Questions:**
*   Can you show a clear example of an RNN model, some of the instances it takes as input, and also exemplify abductive and contrastive explanations for some of the model's decisions? The current examples (Example 1, 2, 3) are all for automata, while a significant portion of the paper focuses on the complexity of RNNs and Transformers. Providing such an example, even a conceptual one, would greatly enhance the paper's accessibility and relevance to the ICLR audience.
*   Are the algorithms originally proposed for abductive (AXp) and counterfactual (CXp) computation in non-sequential models directly usable in this sequential setting, perhaps with modifications? If not, for the cases where polynomial-time solutions *do* exist (e.g., counterfactuals for interval and register automata), how should one compute them in practice? The paper outlines theoretical reductions (e.g., to shortest path problems), but a more detailed algorithmic description (e.g., pseudo-code, concrete steps for constructing the weighted graph for a given automaton and input) would be beneficial.
*   Can you summarize the differences between the complexity of these problems for non-sequential and sequential models in a more consolidated manner? This way, can you explicitly contrast your results with what is already known to hold in general for non-sequential models (e.g., MLPs, decision trees, perceptrons, as referenced in the Introduction on page 1)? A comparison table or a dedicated subsection outlining these contrasts would significantly help contextualize your contributions within the broader FXAI literature.
*   Why do you need to represent `k` in unary in the informal definition of a counterfactual explanation (footnote 2 on page 4: "Represented in unary, i.e., as the string 1k")? It doesn't seem to be explicitly used or justified in the main text's algorithmic complexity analyses, which are typically based on binary representations of numbers unless otherwise specified. Clarifying the implication of this choice for the stated complexity bounds would be helpful.

**Rating:**
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Quantized Local Independence Discovery for Fine-Grained Causal Dynamics Learning in Reinforcement Learning

reviews.summary: This paper considers using the subgraph decomposition method under certain event conditions to enhance the generalization and robustness of the. The first step is to find the partition of events. Then, based on the partitions, a causal discovery method is applied to find the causal graph.
reviews.strengths: This paper presents an interesting idea, which suggests that causal relationships can be changed with variations in the conditional event. This is an issue worth discussing, especially in dynamic systems. The writing in this paper is easy to follow, and the method is supported by thorough experimental work.
reviews.weaknesses: See the questions below. If the authors can solve my concerns, I would like to raise my score.
reviews.questions:
* How can we understand the concept of 'event'? Is it akin to a specific state value or an additional variable, similar to the domain ID in domain generalization tasks? Could you provide some examples in the context of reinforcement learning scenarios? 
* Definition 1 may not fully describe an event-conditioned system. What if we consider a situation where changing the event alters the entire causal relationship? Definition 1 only discusses cases where the edges under certain conditions should be a subset of the original edge set. 

* Can the score (equation 4) assist in identifying the causal graph? In Huang et al.'s paper, the score is a direct measure of conditional independence. Does equation 4 also measure conditional independence? In Brouillard et al.'s work, I found that the intervention data are required to support identifiability. Previous works have used score-based methods to discover causal graphs only under certain assumptions (e.g., linear and non-Gaussian Structural Causal Models). However, this paper lacks rigorous assumptions that substantiate identifiability. 

* In equation 5, both h and e_j are learned. How can we ensure a nontrivial result? Specifically, if both h and e_j are learned as the static value 0, how can we avoid trivial outcomes?

* Although there are some positive results in Table 3 shows that considering the subgraph can help to enhance the causal discovery score. I’m still curious about why breaking the full graph into subgraphs by removing unnecessary edges can help enhance the robustness. If the edges are unnecessary, the causal function on the full graph will not consider the causal effect of it.

Summary:
This paper considers using the subgraph decomposition method under certain event conditions to enhance the generalization and robustness of the. The first step is to find the partition of events. Then, based on the partitions, a causal discovery method is applied to find the causal graph.

Strengths:
This paper presents an interesting idea, which suggests that causal relationships can be changed with variations in the conditional event. This is an issue worth discussing, especially in dynamic systems. The writing in this paper is easy to follow, and the method is supported by thorough experimental work.

Weaknesses:
See the questions below. If the authors can solve my concerns, I would like to raise my score.

Questions:
* How can we understand the concept of 'event'? Is it akin to a specific state value or an additional variable, similar to the domain ID in domain generalization tasks? Could you provide some examples in the context of reinforcement learning scenarios? 
* Definition 1 may not fully describe an event-conditioned system. What if we consider a situation where changing the event alters the entire causal relationship? Definition 1 only discusses cases where the edges under certain conditions should be a subset of the original edge set. 

* Can the score (equation 4) assist in identifying the causal graph? In Huang et al.'s paper, the score is a direct measure of conditional independence. Does equation 4 also measure conditional independence? In Brouillard et al.'s work, I found that the intervention data are required to support identifiability. Previous works have used score-based methods to discover causal graphs only under certain assumptions (e.g., linear and non-Gaussian Structural Causal Models). However, this paper lacks rigorous assumptions that substantiate identifiability. 

* In equation 5, both h and e_j are learned. How can we ensure a nontrivial result? Specifically, if both h and e_j are learned as the static value 0, how can we avoid trivial outcomes?

* Although there are some positive results in Table 3 shows that considering the subgraph can help to enhance the causal discovery score. I’m still curious about why breaking the full graph into subgraphs by removing unnecessary edges can help enhance the robustness. If the edges are unnecessary, the causal function on the full graph will not consider the causal effect of it.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

Summary:
This paper introduces a novel approach to dynamics learning in reinforcement learning by leveraging a subgraph decomposition method conditioned on specific "events." The core idea is to move beyond global causal relationships and infer fine-grained, event-specific causal dynamics. The first step involves identifying a partition of the data domain into these meaningful events. Subsequently, for each identified event, a causal discovery method is applied to infer a local causal graph (LCG), which then guides the dynamics model to use only locally relevant variables for prediction. This approach aims to enhance robustness and generalization in model-based RL. The authors propose a practical gradient-based method that utilizes vector quantization to jointly learn the event decomposition and the corresponding LCGs, enabling an end-to-end training process.

Strengths:
This paper presents an interesting and highly relevant idea, which suggests that causal relationships can be dynamic and change with variations in the conditional event. This premise aligns well with real-world scenarios, as highlighted in the introduction with the autonomous driving example where a lane change's impact is contingent on the presence of nearby cars. This is an issue worth discussing, especially in complex and dynamic systems like those encountered in reinforcement learning, where accurately modeling transitions is crucial for robust agents. The writing in this paper is easy to follow, providing a clear exposition of the problem, theoretical formulation, and proposed method. The method is further supported by thorough experimental work across both discrete (Chemical) and continuous (Magnetic) control environments. The experiments demonstrate improved robustness to unseen states and generalization to downstream tasks (Tables 1 and 2), successful identification of meaningful events and recovery of event-specific causal relationships (Figures 4 and 5), and an ablation study on the codebook size (Table 3), all of which lend credibility to the proposed approach.

Weaknesses:
While the paper presents a compelling idea and promising empirical results, several theoretical and practical aspects warrant further clarification or more rigorous treatment. See the questions below. If the authors can solve my concerns, I would like to raise my score.

Questions:
*   How can we understand the concept of 'event'? Is it akin to a specific state value or an additional variable, similar to the domain ID in domain generalization tasks? Could you provide some examples in the context of reinforcement learning scenarios?
    The abstract and Section 3.1 define an event `E` as a "subset of the joint state and action space `X`," where local independence relationships hold. While the autonomous driving example (lane change contingent on nearby cars) provides good intuition, it would be beneficial to elaborate on whether `E` represents a specific *value* or a *range* of values for certain state/action variables. For instance, in the Chemical environment, is an "event" defined by the root node having a *specific color* (e.g., red), or a *set of colors*? In the Magnetic environment, is it the *exact position* of the box, or a *region*? The paper states that the method "discovers such meaningful events" (Abstract), implying `E` is learned implicitly through the latent codebook. Clarifying the nature of this learned `E` – whether it corresponds to explicit, interpretable conditions on `s` and `a` or remains a purely latent construct – would enhance understanding. Also, providing more concrete RL examples beyond the conceptual one from autonomous driving would be helpful.

*   Definition 1 may not fully describe an event-conditioned system. What if we consider a situation where changing the event alters the entire causal relationship? Definition 1, along with Proposition 1 (Monotonicity), only discusses cases where the edges under certain conditions (`E_E`) should be a subset of the original edge set (`E`). This implies that local causal graphs (`G_E`) are always subgraphs of the global causal graph (`G`), meaning relationships can only disappear, not appear or change direction. This formulation is restrictive, as in real-world scenarios, a change in context (event) might introduce new causal links or even reverse existing ones. For example, a switch in a physical system's mode of operation could activate entirely new causal mechanisms. Have the authors considered or observed situations where new causal relationships might emerge under certain events, which the current definition would not capture?

*   Can the score (equation 4) assist in identifying the causal graph? In Huang et al.'s paper, the score is a direct measure of conditional independence. Does equation 4 also measure conditional independence? In Brouillard et al.'s work, I found that the intervention data are required to support identifiability. Previous works have used score-based methods to discover causal graphs only under certain assumptions (e.g., linear and non-Gaussian Structural Causal Models). However, this paper lacks rigorous assumptions that substantiate identifiability.
    The paper cites existing works (Huang et al., 2018; Brouillard et al., 2020) regarding causal graph identifiability in factored MDPs, stating that "the causal graph is uniquely identifiable with oracle conditional independence test or score maximization." However, for the proposed *local* causal graphs and the score function in Equation 4, it is not immediately clear what specific assumptions (beyond the general Assumptions 1-5 in Appendix B.1) guarantee unique identifiability from *observational data*, especially since the "events" themselves are learned. While Theorem 1 discusses identifying a "meaningful context" and "fine-grained causal relationships," it relies on Assumptions 1-5 and "small enough λ." A more explicit discussion on how the score (Eq. 4) inherently serves as a measure of conditional independence for *local* relationships, and what parametric or non-parametric assumptions (e.g., on the dynamics models `p_j` or the underlying SCM) are made to ensure identifiability of the LCGs without intervention data, would significantly strengthen the theoretical foundation.

*   In equation 5, both `h` and `e_j` are learned. How can we ensure a nontrivial result? Specifically, if both `h` and `e_j` are learned as the static value 0, how can we avoid trivial outcomes?
    The concern here is about potential "codebook collapse" or trivial solutions in the vector quantization process, where all embeddings `h` map to a single prototype `e_j`, or all `e_j` converge to identical, possibly zero, values. While Section C.3.1 acknowledges "codebook collapsing, a well known issue in VQ-VAE literature" and mentions observing "unstable" behavior when `K=2`, the paper does not explicitly detail how the current training objective (Eq. 7) or other mechanisms inherently prevent such trivial outcomes. Techniques like codebook reset or stochastic quantization are mentioned as future work, implying they are not currently used. It would be valuable to clarify if trivial solutions (e.g., all codes representing the global graph, or only a subset of codes being utilized) were observed in practice and how the current implementation mitigates these, especially for larger `K`. The instability for `K=2` (Figure 15) points to this being a practical challenge.

*   Although there are some positive results in Table 3 shows that considering the subgraph can help to enhance the causal discovery score. I’m still curious about why breaking the full graph into subgraphs by removing unnecessary edges can help enhance the robustness. If the edges are unnecessary, the causal function on the full graph will not consider the causal effect of it.
    This question probes the specific mechanism by which explicitly learning and utilizing locally relevant variables (i.e., pruning locally unnecessary edges) improves robustness, beyond what a global causal model might achieve. A global causal model (like CDL or GRADER) aims to identify *all* relevant parents `Pa(j)` for `S'_j` and ideally, if an edge is truly "unnecessary" (i.e., has no causal effect), the learned causal function `p(S'_j | Pa(j))` should effectively ignore it. However, in practice, with finite data and model capacity, a global model might still learn spurious correlations or allocate capacity to modeling weak, globally-present but locally-irrelevant dependencies. By explicitly identifying and *removing* these locally irrelevant edges for specific events, the proposed method simplifies the causal structure for that event, potentially making the dynamics model more parsimonious, less prone to overfitting to spurious correlations in specific contexts, and thus more robust to out-of-distribution (OOD) states where these spurious correlations might break down. The examples in the Chemical (noisy nodes are locally spurious) and Magnetic (box position unnecessary in non-magnetic context) environments illustrate this, suggesting that explicit local pruning provides a more targeted and robust form of dependency discarding compared to relying solely on a global model to implicitly filter out locally spurious links.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces a novel approach to reinforcement learning that explicitly incorporates fine-grained causal relationships into dynamics learning. The authors propose a method to discover local independence relationships that hold under specific circumstances, referred to as "events," rather than relying solely on global conditional independences. The core of their approach involves jointly optimizing an L1 regularized maximum likelihood score for dynamics prediction and a vector quantization loss. This joint optimization process aims to simultaneously identify meaningful events and their corresponding event-specific causal graphs (Local Causal Graphs, LCGs). The method leverages a discrete latent variable, learned via vector quantization, to represent the pair of an event and its associated causal relationships. Experimental results on two distinct environments, Chemical and Magnetic, are presented, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method in improving robustness and generalization in model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) compared to several baselines. The paper also provides detailed analyses showing that the method successfully identifies meaningful events and recovers fine-grained causal relationships.

**Strengths:**
1.  **The method looks novel to me.** The integration of vector quantization with causal dynamics learning to discover local independences and identify "events" is a particularly innovative aspect. Unlike prior causal dynamics models that focus on global conditional independences (e.g., CDL, GRADER) or sample-specific dependencies (NCD), this paper's approach of learning a discrete latent codebook to explicitly represent and cluster event-specific causal relationships offers a unique way to understand and leverage fine-grained dynamics. The authors' formulation of decomposing the data domain into subgroups (events) and inferring LCGs for each subgroup through a differentiable, end-to-end trainable mechanism (Fig. 2) presents a distinct contribution to the field of causal RL.

2.  **Overall speaking the identifiability theory looks plausible to me.** The paper provides a theoretical foundation for its approach, particularly in Section 3.4 and Appendix B. Proposition 2 (Score Maximization for LCGs) and Proposition 3 (Optimality of Decomposition) establish that maximizing the regularized maximum likelihood score can identify true LCGs and meaningful events. Furthermore, Theorem 1 on Identifiability suggests that the optimal decomposition discovered by the method indeed captures meaningful contexts exhibiting fine-grained causal relationships. This theoretical grounding strengthens the paper's claims regarding its ability to learn interpretable and robust causal dynamics.

3.  **The paper is well-written and easy to follow.** The clarity of the problem statement in the Introduction (Section 1) and the logical progression through preliminaries to the proposed method in Section 3 make the paper accessible. The use of illustrative figures, such as Fig. 1 comparing different dynamics models and Fig. 2 detailing the overall framework, significantly aids in understanding the core concepts and the proposed architecture. The mathematical formulations are presented clearly, and the experimental setup and results are well-organized, contributing to the overall readability.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  **The quantization technique in eq 5 is not novel. VQVAE and related papers should be cited there.** The use of vector quantization, specifically the `argmin ||h - ej||^2` operation in Equation 5 and the associated quantization loss in Equation 7, is a standard technique widely popularized by works like VQ-VAE (Van Den Oord et al., 2017). While the application of this technique in the context of causal dynamics learning for event identification is novel, the underlying quantization mechanism itself is not. Therefore, specific citations to VQ-VAE and potentially other relevant works (e.g., Ozair et al., 2021; Takida et al., 2022, which are mentioned in Appendix C.3.3) should be included directly in Section 3.3 where the technique is introduced, to properly acknowledge its origins.

2.  **Though overall the identifiability theory is plausible, however, the identifiability of events is unclear to me. See my questions below.** While the theoretical arguments for LCG identifiability and score maximization are presented, the precise mechanism by which the vector quantization process reliably carves out *semantically meaningful events* from the data remains a point of ambiguity. The theory primarily focuses on the identifiability of LCGs *given* a decomposition, but the robustness and interpretability of the decomposition itself, as produced by the VQ module, could benefit from further clarification.

**Questions:**
1.  **Why an event-specific graph has to be constrained to be a subgraph of the underlying G?** Definition 1 (Local Causal Graph) and subsequent discussions (e.g., Proposition 1 on Monotonicity) explicitly state that `G_E ⊆ G`, meaning a Local Causal Graph for an event `E` is a subgraph of the global causal graph `G`. Is this an inherent property of local independences or an inductive bias introduced by the model design? If it's an assumption, what are its implications for discovering truly novel fine-grained causal relationships that might not strictly adhere to a subgraph structure of a pre-defined global graph, or if the global graph itself is complex or unknown?

2.  **Why vector quantization can correctly identify the event?** The paper states in Section 3.3 that "The quantization entails the decomposition of X since each sample corresponds to exactly one of the latent codes." While the `argmin` operation assigns each latent embedding `h` to the nearest prototype vector `e_j` (Eq. 5), the fundamental question is what properties of the encoder `g_enc` or the latent space ensure that this clustering accurately corresponds to meaningful *causal events* rather than arbitrary statistical groupings. Is there a theoretical guarantee or empirical evidence (beyond the qualitative analysis in Figs. 4 and 5) that the learned latent codes `e_z` consistently represent distinct causal contexts, especially given the known challenges of codebook collapse in VQ-based models (Appendix C.3.3)?

3.  **Does the identifiability in Theorem 1 rely on the specific choice of K?** Theorem 1 states that the optimal decomposition identifies meaningful context and holds for `K > 1`. The ablation study in Table 3 shows performance varies with different `K` values (e.g., `K=2` vs `K=16` vs `K=32`), and Appendix C.3.1 mentions instability for `K=2` in some cases. While the paper states that "our method works reasonably well for any choice of K > 1," could the authors elaborate on the theoretical implications or practical guidance for selecting an appropriate `K`? Does the "meaningful context" identification hold equally robustly across all `K > 1` choices, or are there theoretical bounds or heuristics that could inform the choice of codebook size to ensure optimal event identifiability?

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a novel reinforcement learning method by considering causal relationships. Specifically, the authors jointly optimize the l1 regularized likelihood and a vector quantization loss, during which the event and each event-specific causal graph are expected to be identified. Experimental results on two environments show the effectiveness of the proposed method.

Strengths:
1. The method looks novel to me.

2. Overall speaking the identifiability theory looks plausible to me.

3. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.

Weaknesses:
1. The quantization technique in eq 5 is not novel. VQVAE and related papers should be cited there.

2. Though overall the identifiability theory is plausible, however, the identifiability of events is unclear to me. See my questions below.

Questions:
1. Why an event-specific graph has to be constrained to be a subgraph of the underlying G?

2. Why vector quantization can correctly identify the event?

3. Does the identifiability in Theorem 1 rely on the specific choice of K?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper presented a local causal dynamic learning method by introducing meaningful events. It consists of two parts (1) inferencing local causal graph through quantization and (2) predicting next state. Empirical result on two RL environments showed the effectiveness of the proposed learning method. More importantly it also showed identifiability result for the proposed method.
reviews.strengths:
1. The assumption that separating the whole (s,a) space into decomposition is intuitive.
2. This paper provided identifiability result of the proposed method that the optimal decomposition that maximizes the score identifies a meaningful context that exhibits fine-grained causal relationships.
3. The experiments (Chemical and Magnetic) showed the effectiveness of the proposed method.
reviews.weaknesses:
* The theory and the implementation seem not aligned.
  * In theory there are ground truth decomposition which the LCG are the same within partition and different otherwise, however, it seems that for Magnetic, there is a uniform causal graph which does not change locally. 
  * Identifiability results are not reflected in the method. There is no quantitative result that supporting that identifiability is or is not achieved in the experiment. 
  * Unclear relation between ID, OOD setting with the decomposition and the codebook size K (see questions).
* The assumption on the decomposition is not mild. In real world data, the transition function is usually highly complex. This method can only handle the case when different parts in the decomposition have different LCG. However, in real case, all parts may share the same LCG due to the highly complex transition function. Even though the conditional independence relations are the same (same LCG), the transition function itself may be used to further partition the sample space into further small parts. That is saying same LCG but with different $p(s'\vert s,a)$.
reviews.questions:
* Minor presentation issues about the notations.
  * In Sec 3.1, what is the definition of event $\mathcal{E}$?
  * In terms of the score function $\mathcal{S}(\\{\mathcal{G}_z,\mathcal{E}_z\\}_1^K):=\text{sup }\mathbb{E}\left[\text{log}\hat{p}(s' \vert s,a;\mathcal{G}_z) - \lambda \vert \mathcal{G}_z\vert\right]$
    * Which variables are for sup?
    * Which variables are for $\mathbb{E}$?
* In Proposition 3. why $\mathbb{E}\left[\vert\mathcal{G}_z\vert\right] \le \mathbb{E}\left[\vert\mathcal{\hat{G}}_z\vert\right]$.
  * Specifically, in appendix B.2 proof of Proposition 3 why the last equality holds? Can you provide detailed derivation involving the definition of score function $\mathcal{S}(\cdot)$?
* In the theory there is a ground truth decomposition $\\{\mathcal{G}_z,\mathcal{E}_z\\}_1^K$. How can this decomposition be aligned with the experiments? i.e., what are the ground truth decomposition and the corresponding LCGs in both Chemical and Magnetic environment?
* How to measure if the identifiability is achieved during learning process? Some measure of the distance between the learned LCG and the true LCG may be used here.
* For ID setting, the proposed method is not the best, is there any explanation for this?
* How to decide the number of codebook vector K?
* For the case mentioned in Weakness, when dealing with more complex data, if the LCG is shared but the transition function is indeed different among partitions, is the proposed method flexible enough to generalize to those case?
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper presented a local causal dynamic learning method by introducing meaningful events. It consists of two parts (1) inferencing local causal graph through quantization and (2) predicting next state. Empirical result on two RL environments showed the effectiveness of the proposed learning method. More importantly it also showed identifiability result for the proposed method.

Strengths:
1. The assumption that separating the whole (s,a) space into decomposition is intuitive.
2. This paper provided identifiability result of the proposed method that the optimal decomposition that maximizes the score identifies a meaningful context that exhibits fine-grained causal relationships.
3. The experiments (Chemical and Magnetic) showed the effectiveness of the proposed method.

Weaknesses:
* The theory and the implementation seem not aligned.
  * In theory there are ground truth decomposition which the LCG are the same within partition and different otherwise, however, it seems that for Magnetic, there is a uniform causal graph which does not change locally. 
  * Identifiability results are not reflected in the method. There is no quantitative result that supporting that identifiability is or is not achieved in the experiment. 
  * Unclear relation between ID, OOD setting with the decomposition and the codebook size K (see questions).
* The assumption on the decomposition is not mild. In real world data, the transition function is usually highly complex. This method can only handle the case when different parts in the decomposition have different LCG. However, in real case, all parts may share the same LCG due to the highly complex transition function. Even though the conditional independence relations are the same (same LCG), the transition function itself may be used to further partition the sample space into further small parts. That is saying same LCG but with different $p(s'\vert s,a)$.

Questions:
* Minor presentation issues about the notations.
  * In Sec 3.1, what is the definition of event $\mathcal{E}$?
  * In terms of the score function $\mathcal{S}(\\{\mathcal{G}_z,\mathcal{E}_z\\}_1^K):=\text{sup }\mathbb{E}\left[\text{log}\hat{p}(s' \vert s,a;\mathcal{G}_z) - \lambda \vert \mathcal{G}_z\vert\right]$
    * Which variables are for sup?
    * Which variables are for $\mathbb{E}$?
* In Proposition 3. why $\mathbb{E}\left[\vert\mathcal{G}_z\vert\right] \le \mathbb{E}\left[\vert\mathcal{\hat{G}}_z\vert\right]$.
  * Specifically, in appendix B.2 proof of Proposition 3 why the last equality holds? Can you provide detailed derivation involving the definition of score function $\mathcal{S}(\cdot)$?
* In the theory there is a ground truth decomposition $\\{\mathcal{G}_z,\mathcal{E}_z\\}_1^K$. How can this decomposition be aligned with the experiments? i.e., what are the ground truth decomposition and the corresponding LCGs in both Chemical and Magnetic environment?
* How to measure if the identifiability is achieved during learning process? Some measure of the distance between the learned LCG and the true LCG may be used here.
* For ID setting, the proposed method is not the best, is there any explanation for this?
* How to decide the number of codebook vector K?
* For the case mentioned in Weakness, when dealing with more complex data, if the LCG is shared but the transition function is indeed different among partitions, is the proposed method flexible enough to generalize to those case?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

Summary:
This paper presented a novel local causal dynamic learning method by introducing the concept of "meaningful events." The approach, termed QUANTIZED LOCAL INDEPENDENCE DISCOVERY, aims to infer fine-grained causal relationships that hold under specific circumstances, rather than globally. It consists of two main computational parts: (1) inferring event-specific local causal graphs (LCGs) through vector quantization of a discrete latent variable, and (2) predicting the next state using only the relevant variables identified by these LCGs. Empirical results on two reinforcement learning (RL) environments, Chemical and Magnetic, demonstrated the effectiveness of the proposed learning method, particularly in terms of robustness and generalization to unseen states. More importantly, the paper also provides an identifiability result (Theorem 1 and Proposition 3) for the proposed method, theoretically establishing that the optimal decomposition maximizes a regularized maximum likelihood score to identify meaningful contexts exhibiting fine-grained causal relationships.

Strengths:
1.  **Intuitive Assumption of Decomposition:** The core assumption that dependencies between variables often exist only under certain circumstances, necessitating a separation of the whole (s,a) space into meaningful decompositions (events), is highly intuitive and well-motivated. As highlighted in the Introduction, practical scenarios like autonomous driving illustrate this: a lane change's causal effect might be contingent on the absence of nearby cars. By recognizing such event-specific causal relationships, the method aims to enhance robustness to spurious correlations and improve generalization to unseen states, which is a significant advantage over traditional dense or global causal models.
2.  **Strong Theoretical Grounding with Identifiability:** This paper provided a crucial identifiability result (Theorem 1, Section 3.4) for the proposed method. This theoretical contribution suggests that the optimal decomposition that maximizes the score (Equation 4) identifies a meaningful context that exhibits fine-grained causal relationships. This means the method can, in principle, recover event-specific LCGs that are sparser and more accurate under specific conditions, as also supported by Proposition 3 which suggests that the learned LCGs will be sparser than or equal to the true LCGs on average.
3.  **Demonstrated Empirical Effectiveness:** The experiments conducted on the Chemical and Magnetic RL environments clearly showed the effectiveness of the proposed method. As presented in Table 1, the method significantly outperforms various baselines (MLP, Modular, GNN, NPS, CDL, GRADER, and NCD) in downstream tasks requiring fine-grained causal reasoning, especially when faced with out-of-distribution (OOD) states or noisy variables. For instance, in the Chemical (full-fork) environment with 6 noisy nodes, our method achieved an average episode reward of 15.27, substantially higher than the next best baseline, NCD, at 10.95. Similarly, Table 2 highlights its superior prediction accuracy on OOD states, maintaining a high performance even with increasing noise, which implies successful capture of fine-grained causal relationships and improved robustness. The detailed analysis of inferred LCGs in Figures 4 and 5 further supports these claims, showing that the model successfully identifies meaningful contexts and recovers expected causal structures.

Weaknesses:
*   **Misalignment between Theory and Implementation/Observation:**
    *   In theory, the framework posits ground truth decompositions where LCGs are expected to be distinct within different partitions. However, it seems that for the Magnetic environment, the overall causal graph (Figure 7a) is fairly consistent, and the local changes primarily involve the activation or deactivation of a few edges (e.g., box position influence on ball movement, Figure 7c). While these are indeed local independencies, the critique suggests that the *degree* of change in LCGs across events might not always be as dramatically different as the theoretical ideal of entirely distinct LCGs for each partition. This could imply that the method's strength is primarily in identifying *sparse* local graphs rather than fundamentally *different* graph structures.
    *   While the paper provides identifiability results in Theorem 1, these theoretical guarantees are not directly reflected or quantitatively validated in the experimental section. There is no explicit quantitative result or experiment designed to specifically support that identifiability, as defined in the theoretical sections, is or is not achieved in the experimental settings. Although Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) is used in Figure 16 to evaluate local causal discovery, it is not explicitly tied back to the conditions or implications of the identifiability theorem.
    *   The relation between the in-distribution (ID) and out-of-distribution (OOD) settings and the theoretical concepts of decomposition and the codebook size K remains somewhat unclear in the main exposition, requiring reference to the detailed questions below for clarification.
*   **Potentially Restrictive Assumption on Decomposition:** The implicit assumption guiding the definition of events and their decomposition might not be universally mild for highly complex real-world data. The current method primarily handles cases where different parts of the decomposition are characterized by *different LCGs*. However, in highly complex scenarios, it is plausible that various distinct "events" could share the same underlying LCG (i.e., the same conditional independence relations hold), but the transition functions $p(s'|s,a)$ themselves are fundamentally different across these events. While the model architecture *does* allow for this by using `z` (the event code) as an input to the dynamics model `p(s'j | Pa(j,z), z)` (as explained in Section C.2.1), the *discovery mechanism* (driven by the sparsity regularization `λ |Gz|` in Equation 6) implicitly favors events with distinct LCGs. This might limit its applicability in settings where functional heterogeneity exists without corresponding structural (causal independence) heterogeneity.

Questions:
*   **Minor presentation issues about the notations:**
    *   In Section 3.1, the definition of an "event" $\mathcal{E}$ is briefly mentioned. Could you explicitly point to its formal definition? (Refer to Appendix B.1, Definition 4, where $\mathcal{E}$ is formally defined as a subset of the joint state and action space $\mathcal{X}$ where local independence holds, adapted from Context-Set Specific Independence.)
    *   In terms of the score function $\mathcal{S}(\{\mathcal{G}_z,\mathcal{E}_z\}_1^K):=\text{sup }\mathbb{E}\left[\text{log}\hat{p}(s' \vert s,a;\mathcal{G}_z) - \lambda \vert \mathcal{G}_z\vert\right]$ (Equation 4):
        *   Which variables are for `sup`? (As specified in Appendix B.2, Equation 8, the `sup` is over `phi`, representing the parameters of the dynamics model `p_hat`.)
        *   Which variables are for $\mathbb{E}$? (As specified in Appendix B.2, Equation 9, the expectation $\mathbb{E}$ is taken with respect to the true data distribution $p(s,a,s')$.)
*   **Proposition 3 Proof Clarification:** In Proposition 3, the statement is "Then, $\mathbb{E}\left[\vert\mathcal{\hat{G}}_z\vert\right] \le \mathbb{E}\left[\vert\mathcal{G}_z\vert\right]$ holds." This implies that the learned LCGs are on average sparser than or equal to the true LCGs. Could you provide a detailed derivation in Appendix B.2 to clarify why this inequality holds? Specifically, the last equality in the proof of Proposition 3 uses the score difference; a more explicit step-by-step derivation from the definition of the score function $\mathcal{S}(\cdot)$ leading to this inequality would be beneficial for clarity.
*   **Alignment of Ground Truth Decomposition with Experiments:** In the theory, there is a ground truth decomposition $\{\mathcal{G}_z,\mathcal{E}_z\}_1^K$. How can this theoretical decomposition be aligned with the experimental setups? Specifically, what are the ground truth decompositions and the corresponding LCGs in both the Chemical and Magnetic environments? (For Chemical, Section 4.1.1 describes how the root node's color defines contexts like "fork" or "chain" structures (Figure 4c, 6). For Magnetic, it's the objects' colors defining magnetic/non-magnetic events (Figure 7b, 7c). These contexts represent the ground truth events $\mathcal{E}_z$ with their associated LCGs $\mathcal{G}_z$.)
*   **Measuring Identifiability during Learning:** How is the achievement of identifiability measured or assessed during the learning process in the experiments? While Figures 4 and 5 provide qualitative insights into inferred LCGs, and Figure 16 presents Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) as a measure of dissimilarity, is there a more direct quantitative result or analysis to support the practical realization of the theoretical identifiability?
*   **Performance on ID Setting:** For the in-distribution (ID) setting (e.g., `n=0` in Tables 1 and 2), the proposed method is not consistently the best, often showing performance comparable to or slightly below other methods. Is there any specific explanation for this observation? (The paper's strength lies in robustness to OOD states and fine-grained causal reasoning, where its benefits are most apparent. For simple ID tasks, other methods, including dense models, might perform adequately, and the added complexity of discovering local independencies might not yield a significant advantage in these specific cases.)
*   **Determining Codebook Size K:** How is the number of codebook vectors K decided in practice? Is there a principled way to select K, or is it primarily a hyperparameter tuned through empirical evaluation? (Section 4.3 and C.2.1 state that the performance did not vary much for K > 2, and K=16 was used across experiments. This suggests empirical robustness to K, but further guidance on selection would be helpful.)
*   **Flexibility for Same LCG, Different Transition Functions:** Regarding the weakness point about complex data where LCGs might be shared but transition functions differ across partitions: Is the proposed method flexible enough to generalize to such cases? How does the model distinguish between events that have identical LCGs but distinct underlying transition dynamics $p(s'|s,a)$? (As clarified in Section C.2.1 ("Masked dynamics model"), the model explicitly accounts for this by feeding the discrete latent code `z` (representing the event) as an additional input to the dynamics model `p_hat`, allowing it to learn "possibly different transition functions even if the LCG is the same." However, the primary mechanism for *discovering* `z` (the event) through quantization is still heavily influenced by the sparsity regularization on the LCGs.)

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Summary:
This paper proposed a novel method that discovers meaningful events and infers fine-grained causal relationships. They recommend learning a discrete latent variable representing the pair of events and causal graphs via vector quantization. Experimental results demonstrate their method is more robust to unseen states.

1.	The discovery of fine-grained event-based causality is novel and intuitive, and the experimental results also prove the author's point of view.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces a novel approach to dynamics learning in reinforcement learning by explicitly discovering fine-grained causal relationships. The core idea is to move beyond global conditional independences and identify "local independence relationships that hold under certain circumstances referred as event" (Abstract). The proposed method, termed QUANTIZED LOCAL INDEPENDENCE DISCOVERY, achieves this by learning a discrete latent variable that represents the pair of event and event-specific causal relationships via vector quantization. This mechanism enables the model to capture a more granular understanding of the dynamics, leading to improved robustness and generalization in RL tasks. Experimental results, particularly on environments designed to highlight such fine-grained causality, demonstrate that their method is more robust to unseen states and generalizes well compared to prior approaches. The authors also show that their method successfully identifies meaningful events and recovers event-specific causal relationships.

**Strengths:**
1.  **The discovery of fine-grained event-based causality is novel and intuitive, and the experimental results also prove the author's point of view.**
    The paper's central contribution of discovering local, event-specific causal relationships is a significant and intuitive advancement over traditional causal dynamics models. As highlighted in the introduction, prior approaches "tend to overlook local independence relationships that hold under certain circumstances" (Abstract), which is crucial in many practical scenarios, such as the autonomous driving example where lane changes are contingent on the absence of nearby cars. The proposed method directly addresses this by decomposing the data domain into "subgroups which we call events," discovering "local independences under each event," and then employing "only locally relevant variables for prediction" (Section 1, Contributions). This fine-grained understanding is empirically validated in Tables 1 and 2. While all methods show similar performance on in-distribution (ID) training tasks, baselines exhibit a "significant performance drop" on out-of-distribution (OOD) states in the Chemical environment (Table 2), often falling to near random guessing accuracy. In stark contrast, our method "consistently outperforms the baselines by a large margin," demonstrating its superior robustness and generalization, precisely due to its ability to capture these event-specific causal relationships.

2.  **The authors theoretically prove that their grouping method does not depend on the hyperparameter k setting and that meaningful context can be discovered in each group.**
    The theoretical grounding of the method is a notable strength. Proposition 3 states that the decomposition that maximizes the score is optimal and yields true Local Causal Graphs (LCGs) for each event, under specific assumptions. Furthermore, Theorem 1 (Identifiability) formally demonstrates that for any K > 1, the optimal decomposition identifies meaningful contexts that exhibit fine-grained causal relationships. This theoretical insight is supported by the ablation study in Table 3, which investigates the impact of the codebook size K. The results indicate that "our method works reasonably well with any choice of K and consistently outperforms baselines" (Section 4.3). This suggests that the method is indeed robust to the specific choice of K, as long as it allows for multiple distinct events, providing practical confidence in its application.

3.  **Figures 4 and 5 in the experimental results clearly demonstrate the advantages of their event-based method in discovering fine-grained causal relationships.**
    The qualitative analysis of the learned LCGs provides compelling evidence for the method's effectiveness. Figure 4, focusing on the Chemical (full-fork) environment, clearly illustrates how the model adapts to OOD states. While "all codes are used during training (Fig. 4(a)), only a few of them are exploited during the test (Fig. 4(b))," indicating that the model successfully identifies the relevant event for generalization. Specifically, the "most commonly inferred LCG on OOD states during the test corresponds to a fork structure, as shown in Figs. 4(c) and 4(d)," which is the true local causal graph for that context. Figure 5 further highlights the advantage of the event-based approach over sample-specific methods like NCD (Hwang et al., 2023). Our method learns a "proper LCG during training, and the inference is consistent in both ID and OOD states on non-magnetic context" (Fig. 5(b)). Conversely, NCD's "inference is inconsistent between ID and OOD states on the same non-magnetic context" (Figs. 5(c) and 5(d)), illustrating how sample-specific approaches can "incur overfitting" (Section 4.3). This is quantitatively supported by the Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) evaluation in Figure 16 (Appendix C.3.2), where our method consistently outperforms NCD in local causal discovery across various codebook sizes (except K=1, where it degenerates to a global causal model), further solidifying the claim of robust LCG inference.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  **The validation environment for the experiment is somewhat simple.**
    The experimental validation, while effective for demonstrating the core concept, is conducted on environments that are relatively simplified compared to the complexity often encountered in real-world reinforcement learning. The Chemical and Magnetic environments (Section 4.1.1) are "designed to exhibit fine-grained causal relationships on a particular context" and explicitly provide "state variables as the observation to the agent." This structured setup, while useful for isolating the contribution, might limit the direct generalizability of the empirical claims to more complex, high-dimensional, or unstructured observation spaces typical in many advanced RL applications.

2.  **And the assumption of full observables seems to have significant limitations.**
    A key assumption underlying the theoretical framework and experimental design is that "states are fully observable" (Section 2.1). This assumption significantly simplifies the problem by bypassing the challenging and often crucial aspect of representation learning from raw observations (e.g., pixels). While standard in some causal RL literature, it represents a notable limitation for broader applicability, as many real-world RL problems involve partially observable or high-dimensional sensory inputs where learning meaningful state representations is a prerequisite. The authors acknowledge this in their limitations section (Appendix C.3.5), suggesting future work could extend the framework to "high-dimensional observation such as image, where representation learning is crucial."

**Questions:**
1.  **In Table 3, it seems that the larger K is, the better the performance is. What happens if K is increased to equal the number of samples?**
    The paper mentions in Appendix C.3.3 that "As K → ∞, our method recovers NCD, a sample-specific inference method." If K were increased to equal the number of samples, the method would effectively degenerate into a sample-specific approach, where each sample constitutes its own "event." As shown in Section 4.3 and Figures 5(c) and 5(d), sample-specific approaches like NCD are prone to "overfitting" and exhibit "inconsistent" inference on OOD states. Therefore, while Table 3 shows improved performance for K=32 over K=16 in some cases, increasing K indefinitely to match the number of samples would likely lead to a loss of the robustness benefits derived from event-specific clustering, as it would reintroduce the overfitting issues observed with NCD. The value of K lies in finding a meaningful, coarse-grained decomposition of the data domain, rather than assigning each sample to a unique event.

2.  **Intuitively, an event should be a sequence of continuous states and actions. I would like to ask whether Eq. (7) is more inclined to aggregate neighboring states?**
    The paper defines an "event" as a "subgroup" or "decomposition" of the data domain X, where X is the joint state and action space (S x A) (Section 3.2). The mechanism for discovering these events is through vector quantization: "each sample (s, a) is encoded into a latent embedding h, which is then quantized to the nearest prototype vector (i.e., code) in the codebook C" (Eq. 5). The `L_quant` loss (Eq. 7) then updates the encoder `genc` and the codebook `C` by encouraging embeddings to be close to their assigned prototype vectors. This process primarily clusters individual (state, action) pairs based on their similarity in the learned latent space, rather than explicitly aggregating *sequences* of continuous states and actions. While temporally contiguous states and actions might naturally produce similar latent embeddings and thus be assigned to the same event, the mechanism itself does not inherently enforce or prioritize temporal continuity. The "event" in this context refers to a specific *context* or *circumstance* defined by the state-action pair, not necessarily a trajectory segment.

**Rating:** 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposed a novel method that discovers meaningful events and infers fine-grained causal relationships. They recommend learning a discrete latent variable representing the pair of events and causal graphs via vector quantization. Experimental results demonstrate their method is more robust to unseen states.

Strengths:
1.	The discovery of fine-grained event-based causality is novel and intuitive, and the experimental results also prove the author's point of view.
2.	The authors theoretically prove that their grouping method does not depend on the hyperparameter k setting and that meaningful context can be discovered in each group.
3.	Figures 4 and 5 in the experimental results clearly demonstrate the advantages of their event-based method in discovering fine-grained causal relationships.

Weaknesses:
1.	The validation environment for the experiment is somewhat simple, and the assumption of full observables seems to have significant limitations.

Questions:
1.	In Table 3, it seems that the larger K is, the better the performance is. What happens if K is increased to equal the number of samples?
2.	Intuitively, an event should be a sequence of continuous states and actions. I would like to ask whether Eq. (7) is more inclined to aggregate neighboring states?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a gradient-based method for local-independence-based causal discovery, and applies it to the Causal Dynamics Modeling problem in model-based reinforcement learning. Following (Hwang et al. 2023), the basic idea is to partition the state-action space into subsets, each called an "event" in this paper, and then to learn the transition function of the MDP as a collection of event-specific causal models. The main difference from prior work is that the event partitioning in this paper is learned through a clustering procedure over a (jointly-learned) latent embedding of the state-action space, using Eq.7. With two specially-designed MDP environments, the paper experimentally shows that MDP planning powered by dynamics model learned from their method performs more robustly under some out-of-distribution settings. Finally, the paper also presents some theoretical results about the soundness of jointly optimizing the event partitioning scheme and the event-specific causal models.
reviews.strengths: I think the paper touches an interesting and important topic. Causal discovery, for whatever reason we want to do it, is often hindered by the fact that many causal effects only manifest themselves under certain conditions. So, targeting at learning local models that each only works for a specific condition, instead of at learning a generic model, seems to be a promising direction for causal discovery in general. The problem of collaboratively learning the condition-specific models and the conditions that effectively facilitate such model discovery, is however quite challenging, and is attacked in this paper. It is also nice to see that the paper embeds this causal discovery task into model-based RL and experimentally tests its application in this scenario.
reviews.weaknesses:
**(a)** I am not sure about the soundness of the proposed method. The paper claims that they are jointly optimizing both the event decomposition/partitioning scheme and the causal models, but I doubt if the learning of the decomposition scheme is indeed effectively signaled by the impact of the decomposition on the quality of the resulted causal models. Moreover, I doubt if the learning objective designed for optimizing decomposition (Eq.7) may lead to degenerate results. See my question 1 and 2 below for the detailed concerns. Since joint optimization is a main selling point of this paper (without it the theory part becomes largely irrelevant to the experiment part, for example), this is a serious issue that must be clarified.

**(b)** I have concern on the reproducibility of the experiment part. No pseudo-code is provided for the proposed method, and the method description in Section 3.3, 4.1.2, and C.2, missed some important pieces. For example, how is the data sampled (what's the exploration policy being used)? Exactly what is being updated by L_pred and L_quant (the gradient is respective to what)? What are exactly computed for the differentiation?
  
Moreover, the experimentation code is not given either, and the description of the two MDP environments are not detailed enough for third-parties to reproduce the experiment, I'm afraid. For example, how exactly the nodes in the Chemical environment affect each other? How exactly the noise is injected at testing time? In the robot-arm environment, how exactly the "unseen locations" of the box is determined at testing time? In Section C.1.3, it's said that at training time the positions of the box are "randomly sampled within the range of the table", does this mean every location on the table has a positive probability density to be selected at training time (If so, the location of the box at testing time is not really out-of-distribution, even though it may be unseen)?   

**(c)** I have concerns on the theory part too. On one hand, the main theorem seems to only apply to a rather special case (where the causal model can only have two irreducible phases). On the other hand, the lemmas (Prop. 1-3) used for proving the main theorems seem to have flawed statements and proofs. Some important assumptions are not explicitly stated. See my question 3~8 for the details in this regard.

**(d)** The paper may need to better contrast to prior work, especially to (Hwang et al., 2023). It seems (Hwang et al., 2023) is not limited to sample-specific decomposition, but also discusses event-level or event-set-level decomposition. Proposition 1 in the paper under review is essentially a rephrasing of Proposition 4 in (Hwang et al., 2023), for example.

---  post-rebuttal edits ---

After the rebuttal I have less concerns in all the four aspects above, but not to the extent that I'm confident to say that the issues have been resolved. Overall, I raise my recommendation to 5 (marginally reject); I think it may be beneficial to have another round of revision and review for this paper, although I won't strongly object to accepting the current draft right away.
reviews.questions:
1. In Eq.6, will the gradient of L_pred be used to update the codebook $C$ and (parameters in) the embedding model $h$? If I understand correctly, the decomposition scheme mainly depends on the codebook $C$ and the embedding model $h$, and both of them affect L_pred through $e$, via Eq.5 (and $e$ affects L_pred further through $A$). However, how does the gradient propagates through the argmin operator in Eq.5? 

(Suppose the gradient of L_pred does not change $C$ and $h$, then the learning of the decomposition scheme actually does not take into account its consequence on the likelihood score, but is solely based on the gradient of L_quant, in this case the algorithm you proposed is not truly a joint optimization method, although the decomposition and the causal models are indeed learned "in parallel".)

2. I don't quite get the rationale behind Eq.7. It seems to me that the L_quant defined in Eq.7 wants to encourage the feature vector $h$ of the state-action and its corresponding cluster center $e$ to be close to each other. But is this enough to learn a "good" decomposition? As a trivial and degenerate solution, imagine the embedding model h always outputs a constant vector, and all the cluster center $e_z$ in the codebook also equal this constant vector, this will minimize L_quant to zero, but is clearly not what we want. This question is assuming that the answer to Question 1 above is no (that the learning of $C$ and $h$ are not further based on the likelihood loss L_pred).

3. I can't quite follow the proof of Proposition 2. In fact, I'm not sure if the proposition is exactly correct as there is no requirement on the value of lambda at all. In comparison, Lemma 1 requires *small enough* (yet non-zero) lambda. Does Prop.2 also require special lambda values? For continuous state and action spaces, there can be an uncountably infinite number of possible decomposition schemes, each may require a different "small enough" lambda for the regularized likelihood score to work, in this case do we still have a single lambda that works for all decompositions? In general, the role of lambda seems to be subtle yet crucial for the entire theory developed here. 

4. In the proof of Proposition 3, I'm not sure about the inequality at line 4 of the proof -- between the equality sign, why can the likelihood terms $\hat{p}$ in S be dropped?

5. The probability factorization at Line 1 of Page 3 implies that you are assuming all the $S'_j$'s are independent to each other (conditioned on given $s,a$), right? Which propositions proved in the paper need this assumption? Without this assumption, the causal model is not necessarily bipartite, and skeleton learning based on local independence would be not enough, and we perhaps don't have unique identifiability any more? Also, does the environments used in your experiment satisfy this assumption? 

6. Please explicitly and formally state all the external propositions that your proofs crucially depend on and yet are only proved in other literature, such as (Hwang et al. 2023, Prop. 4) and (Brouillard et al. 2020, Thm. 1).

7. You define "local causal model" based on the concept of PA(j; E), but how is PA(j;E) defined? Does the global causal model entail the local models through the induced local independence relationships? In that case a proper *definition* of local causal model would be entirely conditioned on the E-faithfulness assumption, am I right?

8. In the proof of Lemma 1 you mentioned some assumptions and said they are assumed "throughout the paper". Such assumptions should not be placed in the appendix, in the middle of a proof for a proposition. They should at least be given in the statement of the involved propositions, in the main text. 

9. Is your algorithm equivalent to NCD if we set $K=|S \times A|$?
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

Summary:

The main difference from prior work is that the event partitioning in this paper is learned through a clustering procedure over a (jointly-learned) latent embedding of the state-action space, using Eq.7. With two specially-designed MDP environments, the paper experimentally shows that MDP planning powered by dynamics model learned from their method performs more robustly under some out-of-distribution settings. Finally, the paper also presents some theoretical results about the soundness of jointly optimizing the event partitioning scheme and the event-specific causal models.

Strengths:
I think the paper touches an interesting and important topic. Causal discovery, for whatever reason we want to do it, is often hindered by the fact that many causal effects only manifest themselves under certain conditions. **(b)** I have concern on the reproducibility of the experiment part. For example, how is the data sampled (what's the exploration policy being used)? Exactly what is being updated by L_pred and L_quant (the gradient is respective to what)?
